revision.text:
{{日本の寺院 |名称 = 正福寺 |画像 = [[File:Shouhukuji (Toba,Mie) 02.jpg|250px]]
本堂 |所在地 = [[三重県]][[鳥羽市]]松尾町519番地 | 緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 24 | 緯度秒 = 37.4 | 経度度 = 136 |経度分 = 49 | 経度秒 = 35.5 |地図 = Japan#Japan Mie |山号 = 青峯山 |宗旨 = [[真言宗]] |宗派 = [[高野山真言宗]] |寺格 = |本尊 = [[十一面観音]] |創建年 = [[天平勝宝]]年中 |開山 = [[行基]]（伝） |開基 = |中興年 = [[貞享]]年間 |中興 = |正式名 = 青峯山正福寺 |別称 = 青峰さん、嵯峨御所 |札所等 = [[伊勢西国三十三所観音霊場]] 番外
[[東海近畿地蔵霊場]] 第17番
[[東海三十六不動尊霊場]] 第23番
[[三重四国八十八箇所|三重四国八十八箇所霊場]] 第88番 |文化財 = 鳥羽市指定文化財2点 |公式HP = |公式HP名 = }} '''正福寺'''（しょうふくじ）は、[[三重県]][[鳥羽市]]松尾町にある、[[高野山真言宗]]の[[仏教]][[寺院]]。山号は青峯山（あおのみねさん）で、同名の山（海抜336メートル）の頂上付近にある{{Cite web|和書|url=https://isekannon.jp/wp-content/themes/isesaigoku2020/files/images/common/panf.pdf|title=伊勢西国三十三所観音巡礼|publisher=伊勢西国三十三所観音巡礼事務局|accessdate=2024-12-17}}。別名は'''嵯峨御所'''鳥羽市史編さん室 編（1991）：703ページ。 海上守護の[[霊峰]]として漁業関係者の篤い信仰を集め鳥羽市史編さん室 編（1991）：818ページ、信者は[[北海道]][[小平町]]から[[鹿児島県]][[鹿児島市]]まで{{#tag:ref|正福寺に奉納された護摩札などの分析による。|group="注"}}日本中に分布する鳥羽市史編さん室 編（1991）：706ページ。 == 概要 == 鳥羽市と[[志摩市]]の境界を成す青峰山の山頂（天跡山）よりやや下った谷あいに位置し、'''青峰さん'''の名で親しまれている。[[曹洞宗]]の寺院が多い[[志摩地方|鳥羽志摩]]鳥羽市史編さん室 編（1991）：999ページでは珍しい[[真言宗]]の寺院である。 === 境内 === 30年かけて建造されたと伝わる[[#大門|山門]]の内側には[[金堂]]・[[聖天]]堂・[[大師堂]]・[[弁天堂]]・如意輪堂・客殿・[[庫裡]]などがある鳥羽市史編さん室 編（1991）：1041ページ。境内にある鐘は自由に撞いてよい近畿日本ツーリスト出版センター（2005）：103ページ。 本堂は1836年に建造[http://www.takenaka.co.jp/majorworks/30050111836-01.html?keepThis=true&TB_iframe=true（竹中工務店）]。 === 本尊 === [[十一面観音]]を[[本尊]]とする。[[#御船祭|御船祭]]の際に[[開帳]]となる。この観音は相差村（おうさつむら、現在の鳥羽市相差町）白浜の海から[[クジラ]]の背に乗って現れた黄金仏である、という伝説がある。この時、クジラがやってきた場所の岬が「鯨崎」となり、観音を乗せたクジラが石化したとされる「鯨石」もある[[三重テレビ放送]]"[http://www.mietv.com/yajikita09/archive/090408/04.html ええじゃないか。平成弥次さん出会い旅/鯨崎遊歩道]"2009年4月8日（2011年5月15日閲覧。）。 その後、婆羅門僧正が来訪し観音像を拝むと像が光り、[[腰蓑]]の漁夫の姿になったという鳥羽市史編さん室 編（1991）：1040ページ。僧正は篤い崇敬の念を抱き、木仏を掘って中に観音像を納めた。 == 歴史 == === 創建の伝説 === 正福寺に伝わる『正福寺縁起』によれば、[[奈良時代]]の[[天平]]期に、僧の[[行基]]が建立したとされる。行基は[[天平勝宝]]年中（[[749年]] - [[757年]]）に[[伊勢神宮]]へ[[勅]]を奉じて参詣したところ、以下のような[[神託]]を賜ったという鳥羽市史編さん室 編（1991）：1039ページ。 {{Cquote|これより[[南東|巽]]の方に霊場あり、天朗峯（あおのみね）と名づく。宜しく[[伽藍]]を建てよ、必ず広大無辺の利益あるべし。}} [[大同 (日本)|大同]]元年（[[806年]]）に[[唐]]から[[空海]]が帰国、翌・大同2年（[[807年]]）に[[平城天皇]]の[[綸旨]]を受け、真言宗の寺院に改められた。 === 衰退と再興 === [[中世]]には荒廃するも、[[貞享]]年間頃に僧・弁意の手により再興した。その後、「青峰に参ると風雨の難を免れる」という[[#青峰信仰|青峰信仰]]が広がり、[[江戸時代]]中期には[[廻船]]業の隆盛により正福寺も栄えた。特に[[文化 (元号)|文化]]・[[文政]]期（[[1804年]] - [[1831年]]）に最盛期を迎え、[[尾張国|尾張]]や[[大阪|大坂]]、[[伊勢市|伊勢]]、[[津市|津]]から[[鳥羽港]]や[[的矢湾#概要|的矢港]]へ入港した廻船・[[問屋]]によって支えられた。奉納の世話は地元の鳥羽・[[磯部町的矢|的矢]]・[[河崎 (伊勢市)|河崎]]・千賀（せんが）・堅子の住民が行っていた鳥羽市史編さん室 編（1991）：709ページ。 鳥羽市指定文化財となっている石灯籠は[[御影石]]製の高さ7[[メートル|m]]の常夜灯であり、「[[天保]]八[[丁酉]]年五月 海上安全」の銘文が刻まれている鳥羽市史編さん室 編（1991）：1125ページ。これは[[1837年]]6月のことで、[[水野忠邦]]失脚からおよそ2年後にあたり、海運史上では同時代の転変を示す唯一の具体例であり、貴重なものである。 === 現代 === 現在も信仰は続いているが、その形態は大きく変化した。以前は団体による参拝、登山をして泊りがけで参拝することが一般的だったものが、現在は家族などの小集団で自動車により訪問する人が多くなった名古屋市立大学地域研究ゼミ（2004）。住職は背景に船のトン数が小さくなったこともあると分析している。 [[2016年]]（平成28年）[[5月25日]]、[[第42回先進国首脳会議]]（伊勢志摩サミット）を前に来日中だった[[カナダ]]の[[カナダ首相|首相]]・[[ジャスティン・トルドー]]が妻の[[ソフィー・グレゴワール]]とともに青峰山を登り、正福寺境内を散策するなどして[[結婚記念日]]に合わせた休暇を楽しんだ「カナダ首相は夫婦で山登り 結婚記念日を満喫」[[中日新聞]]2016年5月26日付朝刊。 == 青峰信仰と御船祭 == 信仰者は地元の三重県をはじめとして[[愛知県]]や[[静岡県]]を中心に日本中に広がっている。 === 青峰信仰 === [[File:Anori Gyoko.jpg|thumb|250px|青峰信仰（三重県志摩市）
画像中央の漁船が青峰山の紺色の旗を掲げている。]] 青峰山が海からの見通しが良く、航行目標・距離の捕捉に役立つことから漁業関係者や船員の信仰を集めている{{Cite web|和書|author=山川芳洋|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150715021742/http://www2.ocn.ne.jp/~amagaku/amagaku15.htm|title=海女学15 青峰信仰|accessdate=2019-11-04|quote=Internet Archiveによる2015年7月15日時点のアーカイブページ。}}。[[1988年]]（昭和63年）現在、本堂と聖天堂を結ぶ回廊には、奉納された[[絵馬]]105点、[[護摩]]札36点が掲げられている。多くは「[[海難]]絵馬」というもので、遭難を免れた漁民が納めたものである。現在では、絵馬の代わりに[[進水式]]の時の船を撮った写真を奉納することが一般的となり、[[漁船]]に乗る人だけでなく、[[ヨット]]や[[ボート]]に乗る人の奉納も増加しているという。信仰の形態としては、正福寺への参詣はもちろん、同寺の名が入った紺地の旗を船に掲げ、お札を船に付ける、という行為が見られる。また、相差の海女はお札を首から提げている。 [[愛知県]][[名古屋市]][[南区 (名古屋市)|南区]]では、海上安全・水難防止を願う「青峯山観音菩薩像」が各地に祀られている{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.nagoya.jp/minami/page/0000001459.html|title=名古屋市：青峯山観音（あおみねさんかんのん）（南区）|publisher=南区役所区政部地域力推進室生涯学習担当|date=2019-05-08|accessdate=2019-11-04}}。本来は船の出入りする[[入り江]]や[[新田]]の堤防上に祀られていたが、多くは寺社の境内に移されている。 === 御船祭 === '''御船祭'''（おふなまつり）は、青峰山正福寺で[[1月18日 (旧暦)|旧暦1月18日]]に開催される祭りで同寺の[[お会式|大会式]]（だいえしき）である磯部町史編纂委員会 編（1997）：823ページ。海上守護を祈願する漁業関係者を始め、[[ハイキング]]を楽しむ人も祭りに訪れる片山健生「快晴の下漁師参拝 鳥羽の正福寺で御船祭」[[中日新聞]]2014年2月18日付朝刊、伊勢志摩版18ページ。 前日から籠り堂に詰めて一夜を明かし[[祈祷]]を受け、祈祷札や青峯旗を授与され、各自の船や家に祀る。下山後には下向祝いを山麓の旅館や食堂で行う者もいる。山門の内外に展開する[[屋台|露店]]で弾き猿猿弾きとも言う。やねじり菓子を買い求める慣例がある。[[境内]]は日本全国から奉納された色彩豊かな[[大漁旗]]が埋め尽くし、山中でありながら漁港さながらの賑わいを見せる。 祭り当日には、自動車の一方通行が実施され、鳥羽市側の松尾道から上り、志摩市側の的矢道から下ることになる。志摩市の[[志摩市立磯部小学校]]では授業を短縮して児童が祭りに参加できるよう配慮がなされるが、正福寺を[[学区]]に持つ鳥羽市立加茂小学校では平常通りの授業が行われる。 == 文化財 == 以下の2点が鳥羽市指定文化財となっている。 {| class="wikitable" !  !!指定名称!!指定種別!!時代!!指定日!!解説 |- |1 |青峯山 正福寺大門 |[[有形文化財]]（建造物） |江戸時代（伝・[[文政]]13年＝[[1830年]]） |rowspan="2"|[[1972年]]（昭和47年）[[5月24日]] |「[[鳥羽藩]]に過ぎたるもの」と呼ばれた山門。 |- |2 |青峯山 正福寺石灯籠 |[[有形民俗文化財]] |江戸時代（[[天保]]8年＝[[1837年]]） |信者の献灯による常夜灯。 |} === 大門 === [[File:Shouhukuji (Toba,Mie) 01.jpg|thumb|250px|大門]] 定説では、的矢（現在の志摩市磯部町的矢）の棟梁・中村九造が文政13年（1830年）に建てたものとされているが、[[神宮文庫]]が所蔵する天保5年（[[1834年]]）の『磯部まいり』には「小屋の柵一面に彫物の柱おびただしく積み重ね」という記述があり、食い違いがある鳥羽市史編さん室 編（1991）：1124ページ。柱の彫刻には[[イセエビ]]と鯉が隠れている鳥羽市観光課"[http://www.tobakanko.jp/modules/know/index.php?p=62 鳥羽市観光情報サイト - 知る/青峯山正福寺]"（2011年5月12日閲覧。）。 == 交通 == {{See also|青峰山#青峰道}} 正福寺へ公共交通を利用して訪れる場合、寺の前まで到達する鉄道や路線バスはないため、麓の鉄道駅から登山することになる。 鳥羽市側からは、[[近畿日本鉄道|近鉄]][[近鉄志摩線|志摩線]] [[松尾駅 (三重県)|松尾駅]]から松尾道を利用し、徒歩で約1時間。志摩市側からは、同線[[沓掛駅]]から磯部道を利用し、徒歩で約1時間。志摩市側からは[[五知駅]]から五知道を利用する方法もあるが、五知道は[[磯部町五知|五知]]の住民以外が利用することは少ない三重県教育委員会 編（1986）：210〜212ページ。 自動車・タクシーを利用する場合は、鳥羽市側の松尾道{{#tag:ref|[[1962年]]（昭和37年）に開通した新道三重県教育委員会 編（1986）：213〜224ページ。徒歩登山用の旧道が別にある。|group="注"}}か志摩市側の的矢道を利用する。山門前に[[駐車場]]がある。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === === 出典 === {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == * 磯部町史編纂委員会 編『磯部町史 上巻』磯部町、1997年9月1日、1356pp. * 『'05-'06 伊勢 鳥羽 志摩 松阪』ツーリスト情報版255、近畿日本ツーリスト出版センター、2005年3月31日、167pp. ISBN 4-87638-755-9 * 鳥羽市史編さん室 編『鳥羽市史 下巻』鳥羽市役所、1991年3月25日、1347pp. * [[名古屋市立大学]]地域研究ゼミ（2004年）"[http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf 海女という生き方]"赤嶺淳研究室共同調査報告 * [[三重県教育委員会]] 編『伊勢街道 朝熊岳道・二見道・磯部道・青峰道・鳥羽道 ―歴史の道調査報告書―』三重県教育委員会、1986年3月31日、281pp. == 関連項目 == * [[常安寺 (鳥羽市)]] == 外部リンク == * [http://www.kirari1000.com/www.kirari1000.com.base_data.base_data.phpQkirari_cd=03659.html 青峰山正福寺と御船祭] - [[伊勢志摩きらり千選]]（財団法人伊勢志摩国立公園協会） {{デフォルトソート:しようふくし}} [[Category:三重県の寺]] [[Category:高野山真言宗の寺院]] [[Category:日本の水運]] [[Category:鳥羽市の歴史]] [[Category:三重県内の市町村指定有形文化財]] [[Category:江戸時代の建築]] [[Category:三重四国八十八箇所]] [[Category:三重県の漁業]] [[Category:鳥羽市の建築物]] [[Category:東海三十六不動尊霊場]]

{{infobox religious building | Name = Shōfuku-ji | image = [[File:Shouhukuji (Toba,Mie) 02.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall | location = {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} Matsuo-cho 519 | LatitudeDegrees = 34 | LatitudeMinutes = 24 | LatitudeSeconds = 37.4 | LongitudeDegrees = 136 | LongitudeMinutes = 49 | LongitudeSeconds = 35.5 | Map = Japan#Japan Mie | MountainName = Aonomine-san | Sect = {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗}} | Subsect = Kōyasan Shingon | TempleRank = | Principaldeity = {{ill|Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} | YearFounded = During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era | Founder = {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}} (traditionally attributed) | Restorer = During the Jōkyō era | Alias = Aonomine-san, Saga Palace | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|伊勢西国三十三所観音霊場}} (Non-numbered Temple)
{{ill|Tōkai-Kinki Jizō Pilgrimage|ja|東海近畿地蔵霊場}} (No. 17)
{{ill|Tōkai 36 Fudō Pilgrimage|ja|東海三十六不動尊霊場}} (No. 23)
{{ill|Mie Shikoku 88 Temples|ja|三重四国八十八箇所}} (No. 88) | CulturalProperty = Two Toba City-designated cultural properties | OfficialWebsite = }} '''Shōfuku-ji''' (正福寺) is a Buddhist temple of the {{ill|Kōyasan Shingon|ja|高野山真言宗}} sect, located in {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. Its mountain name is Aonomine-san (青峯山), situated near the summit of a 336-meter-high mountain of the same name.{{Cite web|url=https://isekannon.jp/wp-content/themes/isesaigoku2020/files/images/common/panf.pdf|title=Ise Saigoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|publisher=Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage Office|access-date=December 17, 2024}} It is also known by the alias "Saga Palace." Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 703 The temple is a sacred site for maritime safety, attracting fervent devotion from those connected to the fishing industry.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 818 Worshippers range from {{ill|Obira Town|ja|小平町}}, Hokkaido, to {{ill|Kagoshima City|ja|鹿児島市}}, Kagoshima.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 706 == Overview == Shōfuku-ji is located in a valley slightly below the peak of Aonomine-san (known as Tenato-yama) at the border of {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}} and {{ill|Shima City|ja|志摩市}}. Commonly referred to as "Aonomine-san," it is a rare {{ill|Shingon Buddhist temple|ja|真言宗}} in the {{ill|Shima Peninsula|ja|志摩地方}}, an area dominated by {{ill|Sōtō Zen|ja|曹洞宗}} temples.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 999 === Grounds === Within the mountain gate (constructed over 30 years, according to legend), there are several buildings, including the Main Hall (Kondō), Shōten Hall, Daishi Hall, Benzaiten Hall, Nyoirin Hall, Guest Hall, and the kitchen area.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1041 Visitors are allowed to ring the temple bell freely.Kinki Nippon Tourist Publishing Center (2005): p. 103 The Main Hall was built in 1836.[http://www.takenaka.co.jp/majorworks/30050111836-01.html?keepThis=true&TB_iframe=true (Takenaka Corporation)] === Principal deity === The temple enshrines the {{ill|Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} as its principal deity. The statue is revealed during the {{ill|Ofune Festival|ja|御船祭}}. According to legend, the Kannon statue was a golden Buddha that appeared on the back of a whale from the sea off {{ill|Ousatsu|ja|相差村}} (present-day {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, Osatsu). The site where the whale surfaced is known as "Whale Cape," and the whale itself is said to have turned to stone, now referred to as "Whale Stone."{{Cite web|url=http://www.mietv.com/yajikita09/archive/090408/04.html|title=Whale Cape Promenade|publisher=Mie TV|access-date=May 15, 2011}} The statue was later visited by a high-ranking monk known as Baramon Sōjō, who carved a wooden image to enshrine the luminous statue within.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1040 == History == === Legendary Origins === According to the "Shōfuku-ji Engi," the temple was founded during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era (749–757), Gyōki received a divine oracle at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, instructing him to build a temple at "Tenrō Mine" (青峯), promising boundless spiritual benefits.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1039 In 806 CE, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}} returned from Tang China and, in 807 CE, under imperial edict, converted the temple to the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗}}. === Decline and Revival === After periods of decline, the temple was restored during the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享年間}} by the monk Ben’i. The belief that "pilgrimage to Aonomine protects against storms" spread, leading to a revival supported by the maritime trade of the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Shōfuku-ji reached its peak during the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1831), supported by merchants and seafarers from regions like Owari, Osaka, Ise, and Toba. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1125 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|ja|ジャスティン・トルドー}} visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|ja|第42回先進国首脳会議}} in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either {{ill|Matsuo Station|ja|松尾駅}} or {{ill|Kutsukake Station|ja|沓掛駅}}, both served by {{ill|Kintetsu Shima Line|ja|近鉄志摩線}}. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. The belief that "pilgrimage to Aonomine protects against storms" spread, leading to a revival supported by the maritime trade of the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Shōfuku-ji reached its peak during the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1831), supported by merchants and seafarers from regions like Owari, Osaka, Ise, and Toba. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1125 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|ja|ジャスティン・トルドー}} visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|ja|第42回先進国首脳会議}} in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either {{ill|Matsuo Station|ja|松尾駅}} or {{ill|Kutsukake Station|ja|沓掛駅}}, both served by {{ill|Kintetsu Shima Line|ja|近鉄志摩線}}. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. {{infobox religious building | Name = Shōfuku-ji | image = [[File:Shouhukuji (Toba,Mie) 02.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall | location = {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} Matsuo-cho 519 | LatitudeDegrees = 34 | LatitudeMinutes = 24 | LatitudeSeconds = 37.4 | LongitudeDegrees = 136 | LongitudeMinutes = 49 | LongitudeSeconds = 35.5 | Map = Japan#Japan Mie | MountainName = Aonomine-san | Sect = {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗}} | Subsect = Kōyasan Shingon | TempleRank = | Principaldeity = {{ill|Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} | YearFounded = During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era | Founder = {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}} (traditionally attributed) | Restorer = During the Jōkyō era | Alias = Aonomine-san, Saga Palace | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|伊勢西国三十三所観音霊場}} (Non-numbered Temple)
{{ill|Tōkai-Kinki Jizō Pilgrimage|ja|東海近畿地蔵霊場}} (No. 17)
{{ill|Tōkai 36 Fudō Pilgrimage|ja|東海三十六不動尊霊場}} (No. 23)
{{ill|Mie Shikoku 88 Temples|ja|三重四国八十八箇所}} (No. 88) | CulturalProperty = Two Toba City-designated cultural properties | OfficialWebsite = }} '''Shōfuku-ji''' (正福寺) is a Buddhist temple of the {{ill|Kōyasan Shingon|ja|高野山真言宗}} sect, located in {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. Its mountain name is Aonomine-san (青峯山), situated near the summit of a 336-meter-high mountain of the same name.{{Cite web|url=https://isekannon.jp/wp-content/themes/isesaigoku2020/files/images/common/panf.pdf|title=Ise Saigoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|publisher=Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage Office|access-date=December 17, 2024}} It is also known by the alias "Saga Palace." Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 703 The temple is a sacred site for maritime safety, attracting fervent devotion from those connected to the fishing industry.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 818 Worshippers range from {{ill|Obira Town|ja|小平町}}, Hokkaido, to {{ill|Kagoshima City|ja|鹿児島市}}, Kagoshima.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 706 == Overview == Shōfuku-ji is located in a valley slightly below the peak of Aonomine-san (known as Tenato-yama) at the border of {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}} and {{ill|Shima City|ja|志摩市}}. Commonly referred to as "Aonomine-san," it is a rare {{ill|Shingon Buddhist temple|ja|真言宗}} in the {{ill|Shima Peninsula|ja|志摩地方}}, an area dominated by {{ill|Sōtō Zen|ja|曹洞宗}} temples.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 999 === Grounds === Within the mountain gate (constructed over 30 years, according to legend), there are several buildings, including the Main Hall (Kondō), Shōten Hall, Daishi Hall, Benzaiten Hall, Nyoirin Hall, Guest Hall, and the kitchen area.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1041 Visitors are allowed to ring the temple bell freely.Kinki Nippon Tourist Publishing Center (2005): p. 103 The Main Hall was built in 1836.[http://www.takenaka.co.jp/majorworks/30050111836-01.html?keepThis=true&TB_iframe=true (Takenaka Corporation)] === Principal deity === The temple enshrines the {{ill|Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} as its principal deity. The statue is revealed during the {{ill|Ofune Festival|ja|御船祭}}. According to legend, the Kannon statue was a golden Buddha that appeared on the back of a whale from the sea off {{ill|Ousatsu|ja|相差村}} (present-day {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, Osatsu). The site where the whale surfaced is known as "Whale Cape," and the whale itself is said to have turned to stone, now referred to as "Whale Stone."{{Cite web|url=http://www.mietv.com/yajikita09/archive/090408/04.html|title=Whale Cape Promenade|publisher=Mie TV|access-date=May 15, 2011}} The statue was later visited by a high-ranking monk known as Baramon Sōjō, who carved a wooden image to enshrine the luminous statue within.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1040 == History == === Legendary Origins === According to the "Shōfuku-ji Engi," the temple was founded during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era (749–757), Gyōki received a divine oracle at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, instructing him to build a temple at "Tenrō Mine" (青峯), promising boundless spiritual benefits.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1039 In 806 CE, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}} returned from Tang China and, in 807 CE, under imperial edict, converted the temple to the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗}}. === Decline and Revival === After periods of decline, the temple was restored during the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享年間}} by the monk Ben’i. The belief that "pilgrimage to Aonomine protects against storms" spread, leading to a revival supported by the maritime trade of the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Shōfuku-ji reached its peak during the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1831), supported by merchants and seafarers from regions like Owari, Osaka, Ise, and Toba. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1125 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|ja|ジャスティン・トルドー}} visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|ja|第42回先進国首脳会議}} in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either {{ill|Matsuo Station|ja|松尾駅}} or {{ill|Kutsukake Station|ja|沓掛駅}}, both served by {{ill|Kintetsu Shima Line|ja|近鉄志摩線}}. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. {{ill|Mie Shikoku 88 Temples|ja|三重四国八十八箇所}} (No. 88) | CulturalProperty = Two Toba City-designated cultural properties | OfficialWebsite = }} '''Shōfuku-ji''' (正福寺) is a Buddhist temple of the {{ill|Kōyasan Shingon|ja|高野山真言宗}} sect, located in {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. Its mountain name is Aonomine-san (青峯山), situated near the summit of a 336-meter-high mountain of the same name.{{Cite web|url=https://isekannon.jp/wp-content/themes/isesaigoku2020/files/images/common/panf.pdf|title=Ise Saigoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|publisher=Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage Office|access-date=December 17, 2024}} It is also known by the alias "Saga Palace." Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 703 The temple is a sacred site for maritime safety, attracting fervent devotion from those connected to the fishing industry.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 818 Worshippers range from {{ill|Obira Town|ja|小平町}}, Hokkaido, to {{ill|Kagoshima City|ja|鹿児島市}}, Kagoshima.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 706 == Overview == Shōfuku-ji is located in a valley slightly below the peak of Aonomine-san (known as Tenato-yama) at the border of {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}} and {{ill|Shima City|ja|志摩市}}. Commonly referred to as "Aonomine-san," it is a rare {{ill|Shingon Buddhist temple|ja|真言宗}} in the {{ill|Shima Peninsula|ja|志摩地方}}, an area dominated by {{ill|Sōtō Zen|ja|曹洞宗}} temples.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 999 === Grounds === Within the mountain gate (constructed over 30 years, according to legend), there are several buildings, including the Main Hall (Kondō), Shōten Hall, Daishi Hall, Benzaiten Hall, Nyoirin Hall, Guest Hall, and the kitchen area.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1041 Visitors are allowed to ring the temple bell freely.Kinki Nippon Tourist Publishing Center (2005): p. 103 The Main Hall was built in 1836.[http://www.takenaka.co.jp/majorworks/30050111836-01.html?keepThis=true&TB_iframe=true (Takenaka Corporation)] === Principal deity === The temple enshrines the {{ill|Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} as its principal deity. The statue is revealed during the {{ill|Ofune Festival|ja|御船祭}}. According to legend, the Kannon statue was a golden Buddha that appeared on the back of a whale from the sea off {{ill|Ousatsu|ja|相差村}} (present-day {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, Osatsu). The site where the whale surfaced is known as "Whale Cape," and the whale itself is said to have turned to stone, now referred to as "Whale Stone."{{Cite web|url=http://www.mietv.com/yajikita09/archive/090408/04.html|title=Whale Cape Promenade|publisher=Mie TV|access-date=May 15, 2011}} The statue was later visited by a high-ranking monk known as Baramon Sōjō, who carved a wooden image to enshrine the luminous statue within.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1040 == History == === Legendary Origins === According to the "Shōfuku-ji Engi," the temple was founded during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era (749–757), Gyōki received a divine oracle at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, instructing him to build a temple at "Tenrō Mine" (青峯), promising boundless spiritual benefits.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1039 In 806 CE, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}} returned from Tang China and, in 807 CE, under imperial edict, converted the temple to the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗}}. === Decline and Revival === After periods of decline, the temple was restored during the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享年間}} by the monk Ben’i. The belief that "pilgrimage to Aonomine protects against storms" spread, leading to a revival supported by the maritime trade of the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Shōfuku-ji reached its peak during the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1831), supported by merchants and seafarers from regions like Owari, Osaka, Ise, and Toba. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1125 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|ja|ジャスティン・トルドー}} visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|ja|第42回先進国首脳会議}} in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either {{ill|Matsuo Station|ja|松尾駅}} or {{ill|Kutsukake Station|ja|沓掛駅}}, both served by {{ill|Kintetsu Shima Line|ja|近鉄志摩線}}. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Shingon Buddhism]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Cultural properties in Japan]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Toba City]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Mie Shikoku Pilgrimage]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Shoufukuji Temple (Toba City)]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[ja:正福寺 (鳥羽市)]]

Commonly referred to as "Aonomine-san," it is a rare {{ill|Shingon Buddhist temple|ja|真言宗}} in the {{ill|Shima Peninsula|ja|志摩地方}}, an area dominated by {{ill|Sōtō Zen|ja|曹洞宗}} temples.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 999 === Grounds === Within the mountain gate (constructed over 30 years, according to legend), there are several buildings, including the Main Hall (Kondō), Shōten Hall, Daishi Hall, Benzaiten Hall, Nyoirin Hall, Guest Hall, and the kitchen area.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1041 Visitors are allowed to ring the temple bell freely.Kinki Nippon Tourist Publishing Center (2005): p. 103 The Main Hall was built in 1836.[http://www.takenaka.co.jp/majorworks/30050111836-01.html?keepThis=true&TB_iframe=true (Takenaka Corporation)] === Principal deity === The temple enshrines the {{ill|Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} as its principal deity. The statue is revealed during the {{ill|Ofune Festival|ja|御船祭}}. According to legend, the Kannon statue was a golden Buddha that appeared on the back of a whale from the sea off {{ill|Ousatsu|ja|相差村}} (present-day {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, Osatsu). The site where the whale surfaced is known as "Whale Cape," and the whale itself is said to have turned to stone, now referred to as "Whale Stone."{{Cite web|url=http://www.mietv.com/yajikita09/archive/090408/04.html|title=Whale Cape Promenade|publisher=Mie TV|access-date=May 15, 2011}} The statue was later visited by a high-ranking monk known as Baramon Sōjō, who carved a wooden image to enshrine the luminous statue within.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1040 == History == === Legendary Origins === According to the "Shōfuku-ji Engi," the temple was founded during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era (749–757), Gyōki received a divine oracle at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, instructing him to build a temple at "Tenrō Mine" (青峯), promising boundless spiritual benefits.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1039 In 806 CE, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}} returned from Tang China and, in 807 CE, under imperial edict, converted the temple to the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗}}. === Decline and Revival === After periods of decline, the temple was restored during the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享年間}} by the monk Ben’i. The belief that "pilgrimage to Aonomine protects against storms" spread, leading to a revival supported by the maritime trade of the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Shōfuku-ji reached its peak during the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1831), supported by merchants and seafarers from regions like Owari, Osaka, Ise, and Toba. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1125 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|ja|ジャスティン・トルドー}} visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|ja|第42回先進国首脳会議}} in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either {{ill|Matsuo Station|ja|松尾駅}} or {{ill|Kutsukake Station|ja|沓掛駅}}, both served by {{ill|Kintetsu Shima Line|ja|近鉄志摩線}}. According to legend, the Kannon statue was a golden Buddha that appeared on the back of a whale from the sea off {{ill|Ousatsu|ja|相差村}} (present-day {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, Osatsu). The site where the whale surfaced is known as "Whale Cape," and the whale itself is said to have turned to stone, now referred to as "Whale Stone."{{Cite web|url=http://www.mietv.com/yajikita09/archive/090408/04.html|title=Whale Cape Promenade|publisher=Mie TV|access-date=May 15, 2011}} The statue was later visited by a high-ranking monk known as Baramon Sōjō, who carved a wooden image to enshrine the luminous statue within.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1040 == History == === Legendary Origins === According to the "Shōfuku-ji Engi," the temple was founded during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era (749–757), Gyōki received a divine oracle at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, instructing him to build a temple at "Tenrō Mine" (青峯), promising boundless spiritual benefits.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1039 In 806 CE, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}} returned from Tang China and, in 807 CE, under imperial edict, converted the temple to the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗}}. === Decline and Revival === After periods of decline, the temple was restored during the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享年間}} by the monk Ben’i. The belief that "pilgrimage to Aonomine protects against storms" spread, leading to a revival supported by the maritime trade of the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Shōfuku-ji reached its peak during the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1831), supported by merchants and seafarers from regions like Owari, Osaka, Ise, and Toba. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1125 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|ja|ジャスティン・トルドー}} visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|ja|第42回先進国首脳会議}} in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either {{ill|Matsuo Station|ja|松尾駅}} or {{ill|Kutsukake Station|ja|沓掛駅}}, both served by {{ill|Kintetsu Shima Line|ja|近鉄志摩線}}. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. Main Hall | location = {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} Matsuo-cho 519 | LatitudeDegrees = 34 | LatitudeMinutes = 24 | LatitudeSeconds = 37.4 | LongitudeDegrees = 136 | LongitudeMinutes = 49 | LongitudeSeconds = 35.5 | Map = Japan#Japan Mie | MountainName = Aonomine-san | Sect = {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗}} | Subsect = Kōyasan Shingon | TempleRank = | Principaldeity = {{ill|Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} | YearFounded = During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era | Founder = {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}} (traditionally attributed) | Restorer = During the Jōkyō era | Alias = Aonomine-san, Saga Palace | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|伊勢西国三十三所観音霊場}} (Non-numbered Temple)
{{ill|Tōkai-Kinki Jizō Pilgrimage|ja|東海近畿地蔵霊場}} (No. 17)
{{ill|Tōkai 36 Fudō Pilgrimage|ja|東海三十六不動尊霊場}} (No. 23)
{{ill|Mie Shikoku 88 Temples|ja|三重四国八十八箇所}} (No. 88) | CulturalProperty = Two Toba City-designated cultural properties | OfficialWebsite = }} '''Shōfuku-ji''' (正福寺) is a Buddhist temple of the {{ill|Kōyasan Shingon|ja|高野山真言宗}} sect, located in {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. Its mountain name is Aonomine-san (青峯山), situated near the summit of a 336-meter-high mountain of the same name.{{Cite web|url=https://isekannon.jp/wp-content/themes/isesaigoku2020/files/images/common/panf.pdf|title=Ise Saigoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|publisher=Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage Office|access-date=December 17, 2024}} It is also known by the alias "Saga Palace." Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 703 The temple is a sacred site for maritime safety, attracting fervent devotion from those connected to the fishing industry.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 818 Worshippers range from {{ill|Obira Town|ja|小平町}}, Hokkaido, to {{ill|Kagoshima City|ja|鹿児島市}}, Kagoshima.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 706 == Overview == Shōfuku-ji is located in a valley slightly below the peak of Aonomine-san (known as Tenato-yama) at the border of {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}} and {{ill|Shima City|ja|志摩市}}. Commonly referred to as "Aonomine-san," it is a rare {{ill|Shingon Buddhist temple|ja|真言宗}} in the {{ill|Shima Peninsula|ja|志摩地方}}, an area dominated by {{ill|Sōtō Zen|ja|曹洞宗}} temples.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 999 === Grounds === Within the mountain gate (constructed over 30 years, according to legend), there are several buildings, including the Main Hall (Kondō), Shōten Hall, Daishi Hall, Benzaiten Hall, Nyoirin Hall, Guest Hall, and the kitchen area.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1041 Visitors are allowed to ring the temple bell freely.Kinki Nippon Tourist Publishing Center (2005): p. 103 The Main Hall was built in 1836.[http://www.takenaka.co.jp/majorworks/30050111836-01.html?keepThis=true&TB_iframe=true (Takenaka Corporation)] === Principal deity === The temple enshrines the {{ill|Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} as its principal deity. The statue is revealed during the {{ill|Ofune Festival|ja|御船祭}}. According to legend, the Kannon statue was a golden Buddha that appeared on the back of a whale from the sea off {{ill|Ousatsu|ja|相差村}} (present-day {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, Osatsu). The site where the whale surfaced is known as "Whale Cape," and the whale itself is said to have turned to stone, now referred to as "Whale Stone."{{Cite web|url=http://www.mietv.com/yajikita09/archive/090408/04.html|title=Whale Cape Promenade|publisher=Mie TV|access-date=May 15, 2011}} The statue was later visited by a high-ranking monk known as Baramon Sōjō, who carved a wooden image to enshrine the luminous statue within.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1040 == History == === Legendary Origins === According to the "Shōfuku-ji Engi," the temple was founded during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era (749–757), Gyōki received a divine oracle at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, instructing him to build a temple at "Tenrō Mine" (青峯), promising boundless spiritual benefits.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1039 In 806 CE, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}} returned from Tang China and, in 807 CE, under imperial edict, converted the temple to the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗}}. === Decline and Revival === After periods of decline, the temple was restored during the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享年間}} by the monk Ben’i. The belief that "pilgrimage to Aonomine protects against storms" spread, leading to a revival supported by the maritime trade of the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Shōfuku-ji reached its peak during the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1831), supported by merchants and seafarers from regions like Owari, Osaka, Ise, and Toba. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1125 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|ja|ジャスティン・トルドー}} visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|ja|第42回先進国首脳会議}} in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either {{ill|Matsuo Station|ja|松尾駅}} or {{ill|Kutsukake Station|ja|沓掛駅}}, both served by {{ill|Kintetsu Shima Line|ja|近鉄志摩線}}. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. {{infobox religious building | Name = Shōfuku-ji | image = [[File:Shouhukuji (Toba,Mie) 02.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall | location = {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} Matsuo-cho 519 | LatitudeDegrees = 34 | LatitudeMinutes = 24 | LatitudeSeconds = 37.4 | LongitudeDegrees = 136 | LongitudeMinutes = 49 | LongitudeSeconds = 35.5 | Map = Japan#Japan Mie | MountainName = Aonomine-san | Sect = {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗}} | Subsect = Kōyasan Shingon | TempleRank = | Principaldeity = {{ill|Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} | YearFounded = During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era | Founder = {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}} (traditionally attributed) | Restorer = During the Jōkyō era | Alias = Aonomine-san, Saga Palace | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|伊勢西国三十三所観音霊場}} (Non-numbered Temple)
{{ill|Tōkai-Kinki Jizō Pilgrimage|ja|東海近畿地蔵霊場}} (No. 17)
{{ill|Tōkai 36 Fudō Pilgrimage|ja|東海三十六不動尊霊場}} (No. 23)
{{ill|Mie Shikoku 88 Temples|ja|三重四国八十八箇所}} (No. 88) | CulturalProperty = Two Toba City-designated cultural properties | OfficialWebsite = }} '''Shōfuku-ji''' (正福寺) is a Buddhist temple of the {{ill|Kōyasan Shingon|ja|高野山真言宗}} sect, located in {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. Its mountain name is Aonomine-san (青峯山), situated near the summit of a 336-meter-high mountain of the same name.{{Cite web|url=https://isekannon.jp/wp-content/themes/isesaigoku2020/files/images/common/panf.pdf|title=Ise Saigoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|publisher=Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage Office|access-date=December 17, 2024}} It is also known by the alias "Saga Palace." Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 703 The temple is a sacred site for maritime safety, attracting fervent devotion from those connected to the fishing industry.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 818 Worshippers range from {{ill|Obira Town|ja|小平町}}, Hokkaido, to {{ill|Kagoshima City|ja|鹿児島市}}, Kagoshima.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 706 == Overview == Shōfuku-ji is located in a valley slightly below the peak of Aonomine-san (known as Tenato-yama) at the border of {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}} and {{ill|Shima City|ja|志摩市}}. Commonly referred to as "Aonomine-san," it is a rare {{ill|Shingon Buddhist temple|ja|真言宗}} in the {{ill|Shima Peninsula|ja|志摩地方}}, an area dominated by {{ill|Sōtō Zen|ja|曹洞宗}} temples.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 999 === Grounds === Within the mountain gate (constructed over 30 years, according to legend), there are several buildings, including the Main Hall (Kondō), Shōten Hall, Daishi Hall, Benzaiten Hall, Nyoirin Hall, Guest Hall, and the kitchen area.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1041 Visitors are allowed to ring the temple bell freely.Kinki Nippon Tourist Publishing Center (2005): p. 103 The Main Hall was built in 1836.[http://www.takenaka.co.jp/majorworks/30050111836-01.html?keepThis=true&TB_iframe=true (Takenaka Corporation)] === Principal deity === The temple enshrines the {{ill|Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} as its principal deity. The statue is revealed during the {{ill|Ofune Festival|ja|御船祭}}. According to legend, the Kannon statue was a golden Buddha that appeared on the back of a whale from the sea off {{ill|Ousatsu|ja|相差村}} (present-day {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, Osatsu). The site where the whale surfaced is known as "Whale Cape," and the whale itself is said to have turned to stone, now referred to as "Whale Stone."{{Cite web|url=http://www.mietv.com/yajikita09/archive/090408/04.html|title=Whale Cape Promenade|publisher=Mie TV|access-date=May 15, 2011}} The statue was later visited by a high-ranking monk known as Baramon Sōjō, who carved a wooden image to enshrine the luminous statue within.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1040 == History == === Legendary Origins === According to the "Shōfuku-ji Engi," the temple was founded during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era (749–757), Gyōki received a divine oracle at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, instructing him to build a temple at "Tenrō Mine" (青峯), promising boundless spiritual benefits.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1039 In 806 CE, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}} returned from Tang China and, in 807 CE, under imperial edict, converted the temple to the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗}}. === Decline and Revival === After periods of decline, the temple was restored during the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享年間}} by the monk Ben’i. The belief that "pilgrimage to Aonomine protects against storms" spread, leading to a revival supported by the maritime trade of the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Shōfuku-ji reached its peak during the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1831), supported by merchants and seafarers from regions like Owari, Osaka, Ise, and Toba. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1125 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|ja|ジャスティン・トルドー}} visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|ja|第42回先進国首脳会議}} in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either {{ill|Matsuo Station|ja|松尾駅}} or {{ill|Kutsukake Station|ja|沓掛駅}}, both served by {{ill|Kintetsu Shima Line|ja|近鉄志摩線}}. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. {{ill|Mie Shikoku 88 Temples|ja|三重四国八十八箇所}} (No. 88) | CulturalProperty = Two Toba City-designated cultural properties | OfficialWebsite = }} '''Shōfuku-ji''' (正福寺) is a Buddhist temple of the {{ill|Kōyasan Shingon|ja|高野山真言宗}} sect, located in {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. Its mountain name is Aonomine-san (青峯山), situated near the summit of a 336-meter-high mountain of the same name.{{Cite web|url=https://isekannon.jp/wp-content/themes/isesaigoku2020/files/images/common/panf.pdf|title=Ise Saigoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|publisher=Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage Office|access-date=December 17, 2024}} It is also known by the alias "Saga Palace." Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 703 The temple is a sacred site for maritime safety, attracting fervent devotion from those connected to the fishing industry.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 818 Worshippers range from {{ill|Obira Town|ja|小平町}}, Hokkaido, to {{ill|Kagoshima City|ja|鹿児島市}}, Kagoshima.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 706 == Overview == Shōfuku-ji is located in a valley slightly below the peak of Aonomine-san (known as Tenato-yama) at the border of {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}} and {{ill|Shima City|ja|志摩市}}. Commonly referred to as "Aonomine-san," it is a rare {{ill|Shingon Buddhist temple|ja|真言宗}} in the {{ill|Shima Peninsula|ja|志摩地方}}, an area dominated by {{ill|Sōtō Zen|ja|曹洞宗}} temples.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 999 === Grounds === Within the mountain gate (constructed over 30 years, according to legend), there are several buildings, including the Main Hall (Kondō), Shōten Hall, Daishi Hall, Benzaiten Hall, Nyoirin Hall, Guest Hall, and the kitchen area.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1041 Visitors are allowed to ring the temple bell freely.Kinki Nippon Tourist Publishing Center (2005): p. 103 The Main Hall was built in 1836.[http://www.takenaka.co.jp/majorworks/30050111836-01.html?keepThis=true&TB_iframe=true (Takenaka Corporation)] === Principal deity === The temple enshrines the {{ill|Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} as its principal deity. The statue is revealed during the {{ill|Ofune Festival|ja|御船祭}}. According to legend, the Kannon statue was a golden Buddha that appeared on the back of a whale from the sea off {{ill|Ousatsu|ja|相差村}} (present-day {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, Osatsu). The site where the whale surfaced is known as "Whale Cape," and the whale itself is said to have turned to stone, now referred to as "Whale Stone."{{Cite web|url=http://www.mietv.com/yajikita09/archive/090408/04.html|title=Whale Cape Promenade|publisher=Mie TV|access-date=May 15, 2011}} The statue was later visited by a high-ranking monk known as Baramon Sōjō, who carved a wooden image to enshrine the luminous statue within.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1040 == History == === Legendary Origins === According to the "Shōfuku-ji Engi," the temple was founded during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era (749–757), Gyōki received a divine oracle at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, instructing him to build a temple at "Tenrō Mine" (青峯), promising boundless spiritual benefits.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1039 In 806 CE, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}} returned from Tang China and, in 807 CE, under imperial edict, converted the temple to the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗}}. === Decline and Revival === After periods of decline, the temple was restored during the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享年間}} by the monk Ben’i. The belief that "pilgrimage to Aonomine protects against storms" spread, leading to a revival supported by the maritime trade of the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Shōfuku-ji reached its peak during the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1831), supported by merchants and seafarers from regions like Owari, Osaka, Ise, and Toba. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1125 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|ja|ジャスティン・トルドー}} visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|ja|第42回先進国首脳会議}} in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either {{ill|Matsuo Station|ja|松尾駅}} or {{ill|Kutsukake Station|ja|沓掛駅}}, both served by {{ill|Kintetsu Shima Line|ja|近鉄志摩線}}. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. According to legend, the Kannon statue was a golden Buddha that appeared on the back of a whale from the sea off {{ill|Ousatsu|ja|相差村}} (present-day {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, Osatsu). The site where the whale surfaced is known as "Whale Cape," and the whale itself is said to have turned to stone, now referred to as "Whale Stone."{{Cite web|url=http://www.mietv.com/yajikita09/archive/090408/04.html|title=Whale Cape Promenade|publisher=Mie TV|access-date=May 15, 2011}} The statue was later visited by a high-ranking monk known as Baramon Sōjō, who carved a wooden image to enshrine the luminous statue within.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1040 == History == === Legendary Origins === According to the "Shōfuku-ji Engi," the temple was founded during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era (749–757), Gyōki received a divine oracle at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, instructing him to build a temple at "Tenrō Mine" (青峯), promising boundless spiritual benefits.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1039 In 806 CE, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}} returned from Tang China and, in 807 CE, under imperial edict, converted the temple to the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗}}. === Decline and Revival === After periods of decline, the temple was restored during the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享年間}} by the monk Ben’i. The belief that "pilgrimage to Aonomine protects against storms" spread, leading to a revival supported by the maritime trade of the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Shōfuku-ji reached its peak during the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1831), supported by merchants and seafarers from regions like Owari, Osaka, Ise, and Toba. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1125 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|ja|ジャスティン・トルドー}} visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|ja|第42回先進国首脳会議}} in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either {{ill|Matsuo Station|ja|松尾駅}} or {{ill|Kutsukake Station|ja|沓掛駅}}, both served by {{ill|Kintetsu Shima Line|ja|近鉄志摩線}}. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. {{infobox religious building | name = Shōfuku-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Shouhukuji (Toba,Mie) 02.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall | location = {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} Matsuo-cho 519 | LatitudeDegrees = 34 | LatitudeMinutes = 24 | LatitudeSeconds = 37.4 | LongitudeDegrees = 136 | LongitudeMinutes = 49 | LongitudeSeconds = 35.5 | Map = Japan#Japan Mie | Mountainname = Aonomine-san | Sect = {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗}} | Subsect = Kōyasan Shingon | TempleRank = | Principaldeity = {{ill|Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} | Yearconsecration_year = During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era | Founder = {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}} (traditionally attributed) | Restorer = During the Jōkyō era | Alias = Aonomine-san, Saga Palace | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|伊勢西国三十三所観音霊場}} (Non-numbered Temple)
{{ill|Tōkai-Kinki Jizō Pilgrimage|ja|東海近畿地蔵霊場}} (No. 17)
{{ill|Tōkai 36 Fudō Pilgrimage|ja|東海三十六不動尊霊場}} (No. 23)
{{ill|Mie Shikoku 88 Temples|ja|三重四国八十八箇所}} (No. 88) | CulturalProperty = Two Toba City-designated cultural properties | OfficialWebsite = }} '''Shōfuku-ji''' (正福寺) is a Buddhist temple of the {{ill|Kōyasan Shingon|ja|高野山真言宗}} sect, located in {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. Its mountain name is Aonomine-san (青峯山), situated near the summit of a 336-meter-high mountain of the same name.{{Cite web|url=https://isekannon.jp/wp-content/themes/isesaigoku2020/files/images/common/panf.pdf|title=Ise Saigoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|publisher=Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage Office|access-date=December 17, 2024}} It is also known by the alias "Saga Palace." Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 703 The temple is a sacred site for maritime safety, attracting fervent devotion from those connected to the fishing industry.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 818 Worshippers range from {{ill|Obira Town|ja|小平町}}, Hokkaido, to {{ill|Kagoshima City|ja|鹿児島市}}, Kagoshima.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 706 == Overview == Shōfuku-ji is located in a valley slightly below the peak of Aonomine-san (known as Tenato-yama) at the border of {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}} and {{ill|Shima City|ja|志摩市}}. Commonly referred to as "Aonomine-san," it is a rare {{ill|Shingon Buddhist temple|ja|真言宗}} in the {{ill|Shima Peninsula|ja|志摩地方}}, an area dominated by {{ill|Sōtō Zen|ja|曹洞宗}} temples.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 999 === Grounds === Within the mountain gate (constructed over 30 years, according to legend), there are several buildings, including the Main Hall (Kondō), Shōten Hall, Daishi Hall, Benzaiten Hall, Nyoirin Hall, Guest Hall, and the kitchen area.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1041 Visitors are allowed to ring the temple bell freely.Kinki Nippon Tourist Publishing Center (2005): p. 103 The Main Hall was built in 1836.[http://www.takenaka.co.jp/majorworks/30050111836-01.html?keepThis=true&TB_iframe=true (Takenaka Corporation)] === Principal deity === The temple enshrines the {{ill|Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} as its principal deity. The statue is revealed during the {{ill|Ofune Festival|ja|御船祭}}. According to legend, the Kannon statue was a golden Buddha that appeared on the back of a whale from the sea off {{ill|Ousatsu|ja|相差村}} (present-day {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, Osatsu). The site where the whale surfaced is known as "Whale Cape," and the whale itself is said to have turned to stone, now referred to as "Whale Stone."{{Cite web|url=http://www.mietv.com/yajikita09/archive/090408/04.html|title=Whale Cape Promenade|publisher=Mie TV|access-date=May 15, 2011}} The statue was later visited by a high-ranking monk known as Baramon Sōjō, who carved a wooden image to enshrine the luminous statue within.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1040 == History == === Legendary Origins === According to the "Shōfuku-ji Engi," the temple was founded during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era (749–757), Gyōki received a divine oracle at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, instructing him to build a temple at "Tenrō Mine" (青峯), promising boundless spiritual benefits.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1039 In 806 CE, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}} returned from Tang China and, in 807 CE, under imperial edict, converted the temple to the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗}}. === Decline and Revival === After periods of decline, the temple was restored during the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享年間}} by the monk Ben’i. The belief that "pilgrimage to Aonomine protects against storms" spread, leading to a revival supported by the maritime trade of the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Shōfuku-ji reached its peak during the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1831), supported by merchants and seafarers from regions like Owari, Osaka, Ise, and Toba. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1125 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|ja|ジャスティン・トルドー}} visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|ja|第42回先進国首脳会議}} in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either {{ill|Matsuo Station|ja|松尾駅}} or {{ill|Kutsukake Station|ja|沓掛駅}}, both served by {{ill|Kintetsu Shima Line|ja|近鉄志摩線}}. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1125 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|ja|ジャスティン・トルドー}} visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|ja|第42回先進国首脳会議}} in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either {{ill|Matsuo Station|ja|松尾駅}} or {{ill|Kutsukake Station|ja|沓掛駅}}, both served by {{ill|Kintetsu Shima Line|ja|近鉄志摩線}}. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. Main Hall | location = {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} Matsuo-cho 519 | LatitudeDegrees = 34 | LatitudeMinutes = 24 | LatitudeSeconds = 37.4 | LongitudeDegrees = 136 | LongitudeMinutes = 49 | LongitudeSeconds = 35.5 | Map = Japan#Japan Mie | Mountainname = Aonomine-san | Sect = {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗}} | Subsect = Kōyasan Shingon | TempleRank = | Principaldeity = {{ill|Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} | Yearconsecration_year = During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era | Founder = {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}} (traditionally attributed) | Restorer = During the Jōkyō era | Alias = Aonomine-san, Saga Palace | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|伊勢西国三十三所観音霊場}} (Non-numbered Temple)
{{ill|Tōkai-Kinki Jizō Pilgrimage|ja|東海近畿地蔵霊場}} (No. 17)
{{ill|Tōkai 36 Fudō Pilgrimage|ja|東海三十六不動尊霊場}} (No. 23)
{{ill|Mie Shikoku 88 Temples|ja|三重四国八十八箇所}} (No. 88) | CulturalProperty = Two Toba City-designated cultural properties | OfficialWebsite = }} '''Shōfuku-ji''' (正福寺) is a Buddhist temple of the {{ill|Kōyasan Shingon|ja|高野山真言宗}} sect, located in {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. Its mountain name is Aonomine-san (青峯山), situated near the summit of a 336-meter-high mountain of the same name.{{Cite web|url=https://isekannon.jp/wp-content/themes/isesaigoku2020/files/images/common/panf.pdf|title=Ise Saigoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|publisher=Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage Office|access-date=December 17, 2024}} It is also known by the alias "Saga Palace." Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 703 The temple is a sacred site for maritime safety, attracting fervent devotion from those connected to the fishing industry.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 818 Worshippers range from {{ill|Obira Town|ja|小平町}}, Hokkaido, to {{ill|Kagoshima City|ja|鹿児島市}}, Kagoshima.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 706 == Overview == Shōfuku-ji is located in a valley slightly below the peak of Aonomine-san (known as Tenato-yama) at the border of {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}} and {{ill|Shima City|ja|志摩市}}. Commonly referred to as "Aonomine-san," it is a rare {{ill|Shingon Buddhist temple|ja|真言宗}} in the {{ill|Shima Peninsula|ja|志摩地方}}, an area dominated by {{ill|Sōtō Zen|ja|曹洞宗}} temples.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 999 === Grounds === Within the mountain gate (constructed over 30 years, according to legend), there are several buildings, including the Main Hall (Kondō), Shōten Hall, Daishi Hall, Benzaiten Hall, Nyoirin Hall, Guest Hall, and the kitchen area.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1041 Visitors are allowed to ring the temple bell freely.Kinki Nippon Tourist Publishing Center (2005): p. 103 The Main Hall was built in 1836.[http://www.takenaka.co.jp/majorworks/30050111836-01.html?keepThis=true&TB_iframe=true (Takenaka Corporation)] === Principal deity === The temple enshrines the {{ill|Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} as its principal deity. The statue is revealed during the {{ill|Ofune Festival|ja|御船祭}}. According to legend, the Kannon statue was a golden Buddha that appeared on the back of a whale from the sea off {{ill|Ousatsu|ja|相差村}} (present-day {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, Osatsu). The site where the whale surfaced is known as "Whale Cape," and the whale itself is said to have turned to stone, now referred to as "Whale Stone."{{Cite web|url=http://www.mietv.com/yajikita09/archive/090408/04.html|title=Whale Cape Promenade|publisher=Mie TV|access-date=May 15, 2011}} The statue was later visited by a high-ranking monk known as Baramon Sōjō, who carved a wooden image to enshrine the luminous statue within.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1040 == History == === Legendary Origins === According to the "Shōfuku-ji Engi," the temple was founded during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era (749–757), Gyōki received a divine oracle at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, instructing him to build a temple at "Tenrō Mine" (青峯), promising boundless spiritual benefits.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1039 In 806 CE, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}} returned from Tang China and, in 807 CE, under imperial edict, converted the temple to the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗}}. === Decline and Revival === After periods of decline, the temple was restored during the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享年間}} by the monk Ben’i. The belief that "pilgrimage to Aonomine protects against storms" spread, leading to a revival supported by the maritime trade of the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Shōfuku-ji reached its peak during the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1831), supported by merchants and seafarers from regions like Owari, Osaka, Ise, and Toba. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1125 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|ja|ジャスティン・トルドー}} visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|ja|第42回先進国首脳会議}} in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either {{ill|Matsuo Station|ja|松尾駅}} or {{ill|Kutsukake Station|ja|沓掛駅}}, both served by {{ill|Kintetsu Shima Line|ja|近鉄志摩線}}. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. {{infobox religious building | name = Shōfuku-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Shouhukuji (Toba,Mie) 02.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall | location = {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} Matsuo-cho 519 | LatitudeDegrees = 34 | LatitudeMinutes = 24 | LatitudeSeconds = 37.4 | LongitudeDegrees = 136 | LongitudeMinutes = 49 | LongitudeSeconds = 35.5 | Map = Japan#Japan Mie | Mountainname = Aonomine-san | Sect = {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗}} | Subsect = Kōyasan Shingon | TempleRank = | Principaldeity = {{ill|Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} | Yearconsecration_year = During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era | Founder = {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}} (traditionally attributed) | Restorer = During the Jōkyō era | Alias = Aonomine-san, Saga Palace | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|伊勢西国三十三所観音霊場}} (Non-numbered Temple)
{{ill|Tōkai-Kinki Jizō Pilgrimage|ja|東海近畿地蔵霊場}} (No. 17)
{{ill|Tōkai 36 Fudō Pilgrimage|ja|東海三十六不動尊霊場}} (No. 23)
{{ill|Mie Shikoku 88 Temples|ja|三重四国八十八箇所}} (No. 88) | CulturalProperty = Two Toba City-designated cultural properties | OfficialWebsite = }} '''Shōfuku-ji''' (正福寺) is a Buddhist temple of the {{ill|Kōyasan Shingon|ja|高野山真言宗}} sect, located in {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. Its mountain name is Aonomine-san (青峯山), situated near the summit of a 336-meter-high mountain of the same name.{{Cite web|url=https://isekannon.jp/wp-content/themes/isesaigoku2020/files/images/common/panf.pdf|title=Ise Saigoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|publisher=Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage Office|access-date=December 17, 2024}} It is also known by the alias "Saga Palace." Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 703 The temple is a sacred site for maritime safety, attracting fervent devotion from those connected to the fishing industry.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 818 Worshippers range from {{ill|Obira Town|ja|小平町}}, Hokkaido, to {{ill|Kagoshima City|ja|鹿児島市}}, Kagoshima.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 706 == Overview == Shōfuku-ji is located in a valley slightly below the peak of Aonomine-san (known as Tenato-yama) at the border of {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}} and {{ill|Shima City|ja|志摩市}}. Commonly referred to as "Aonomine-san," it is a rare {{ill|Shingon Buddhist temple|ja|真言宗}} in the {{ill|Shima Peninsula|ja|志摩地方}}, an area dominated by {{ill|Sōtō Zen|ja|曹洞宗}} temples.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 999 === Grounds === Within the mountain gate (constructed over 30 years, according to legend), there are several buildings, including the Main Hall (Kondō), Shōten Hall, Daishi Hall, Benzaiten Hall, Nyoirin Hall, Guest Hall, and the kitchen area.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1041 Visitors are allowed to ring the temple bell freely.Kinki Nippon Tourist Publishing Center (2005): p. 103 The Main Hall was built in 1836.[http://www.takenaka.co.jp/majorworks/30050111836-01.html?keepThis=true&TB_iframe=true (Takenaka Corporation)] === Principal deity === The temple enshrines the {{ill|Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} as its principal deity. The statue is revealed during the {{ill|Ofune Matsuri|ja|御船祭|Ofune Matsuri=Ofune Matsuri|lt=Ofune Festival}}. According to legend, the Kannon statue was a golden Buddha that appeared on the back of a whale from the sea off {{ill|Ousatsu|ja|相差村}} (present-day {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, Osatsu). The site where the whale surfaced is known as "Whale Cape," and the whale itself is said to have turned to stone, now referred to as "Whale Stone."{{Cite web|url=http://www.mietv.com/yajikita09/archive/090408/04.html|title=Whale Cape Promenade|publisher=Mie TV|access-date=May 15, 2011}} The statue was later visited by a high-ranking monk known as Baramon Sōjō, who carved a wooden image to enshrine the luminous statue within.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1040 == History == === Legendary Origins === According to the "Shōfuku-ji Engi," the temple was founded during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era (749–757), Gyōki received a divine oracle at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, instructing him to build a temple at "Tenrō Mine" (青峯), promising boundless spiritual benefits.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1039 In 806 CE, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}} returned from Tang China and, in 807 CE, under imperial edict, converted the temple to the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗}}. === Decline and Revival === After periods of decline, the temple was restored during the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享年間}} by the monk Ben’i. The belief that "pilgrimage to Aonomine protects against storms" spread, leading to a revival supported by the maritime trade of the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Shōfuku-ji reached its peak during the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1831), supported by merchants and seafarers from regions like Owari, Osaka, Ise, and Toba. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1125 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|ja|ジャスティン・トルドー}} visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|ja|第42回先進国首脳会議}} in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either {{ill|Matsuo Station|ja|松尾駅}} or {{ill|Kutsukake Station|ja|沓掛駅}}, both served by {{ill|Kintetsu Shima Line|ja|近鉄志摩線}}. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Shoufukuji Temple (Toba City)]][[Category:Buddhist temples in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Shoufukuji Temple (Toba City)]][[Category:Shingon Buddhism]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Shoufukuji Temple (Toba City)]][[Category:Cultural properties in Japan]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Shoufukuji Temple (Toba City)]][[Category:Toba City]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Shoufukuji Temple (Toba City)]][[Category:Mie Shikoku Pilgrimage]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Shoufukuji Temple (Toba City)]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Shoufukuji Temple (Toba City)]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Shoufukuji Temple (Toba City)]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[ja:正福寺 (鳥羽市)]]

{{infobox religious building | name = Shōfuku-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Shouhukuji (Toba,Mie) 02.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall | location = {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} Matsuo-cho 519 | LatitudeDegrees = 34 | LatitudeMinutes = 24 | LatitudeSeconds = 37.4 | LongitudeDegrees = 136 | LongitudeMinutes = 49 | LongitudeSeconds = 35.5 | Map = Japan#Japan Mie | Mountainname = Aonomine-san | Sect = {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗}} | Subsect = Kōyasan Shingon | TempleRank = | Principaldeity = {{ill|Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} | Yearconsecration_year = During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era | Founder = {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}} (traditionally attributed) | Restorer = During the Jōkyō era | Alias = Aonomine-san, Saga Palace | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|伊勢西国三十三所観音霊場}} (Non-numbered Temple)
{{ill|Tōkai-Kinki Jizō Pilgrimage|ja|東海近畿地蔵霊場}} (No. 17)
{{ill|Tōkai 36 Fudō Pilgrimage|ja|東海三十六不動尊霊場}} (No. 23)
{{ill|Mie Shikoku 88 Temples|ja|三重四国八十八箇所}} (No. 88) | CulturalProperty = Two Toba City-designated cultural properties | OfficialWebsite = }} '''Shōfuku-ji''' (正福寺) is a Buddhist temple of the {{ill|Kōyasan Shingon|ja|高野山真言宗}} sect, located in {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. Its mountain name is Aonomine-san (青峯山), situated near the summit of a 336-meter-high mountain of the same name.{{Cite web|url=https://isekannon.jp/wp-content/themes/isesaigoku2020/files/images/common/panf.pdf|title=Ise Saigoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|publisher=Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage Office|access-date=December 17, 2024}} It is also known by the alias "Saga Palace." Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 703 The temple is a sacred site for maritime safety, attracting fervent devotion from those connected to the fishing industry.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 818 Worshippers range from {{ill|Obira Town|ja|小平町}}, Hokkaido, to {{ill|Kagoshima City|ja|鹿児島市}}, Kagoshima.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 706 == Overview == Shōfuku-ji is located in a valley slightly below the peak of Aonomine-san (known as Tenato-yama) at the border of {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}} and {{ill|Shima City|ja|志摩市}}. Commonly referred to as "Aonomine-san," it is a rare {{ill|Shingon Buddhist temple|ja|真言宗}} in the {{ill|Shima Peninsula|ja|志摩地方}}, an area dominated by {{ill|Sōtō Zen|ja|曹洞宗}} temples.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 999 === Grounds === Within the mountain gate (constructed over 30 years, according to legend), there are several buildings, including the Main Hall (Kondō), Shōten Hall, Daishi Hall, Benzaiten Hall, Nyoirin Hall, Guest Hall, and the kitchen area.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1041 Visitors are allowed to ring the temple bell freely.Kinki Nippon Tourist Publishing Center (2005): p. 103 The Main Hall was built in 1836.[http://www.takenaka.co.jp/majorworks/30050111836-01.html?keepThis=true&TB_iframe=true (Takenaka Corporation)] === Principal deity === The temple enshrines the {{ill|Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} as its principal deity. The statue is revealed during the {{ill|Ofune Matsuri|ja|御船祭|Ofune Matsuri=Ofune Matsuri|lt=Ofune Festival}}. According to legend, the Kannon statue was a golden Buddha that appeared on the back of a whale from the sea off {{ill|Ousatsu|ja|相差村}} (present-day {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, Osatsu). The site where the whale surfaced is known as "Whale Cape," and the whale itself is said to have turned to stone, now referred to as "Whale Stone."{{Cite web|url=http://www.mietv.com/yajikita09/archive/090408/04.html|title=Whale Cape Promenade|publisher=Mie TV|access-date=May 15, 2011}} The statue was later visited by a high-ranking monk known as Baramon Sōjō, who carved a wooden image to enshrine the luminous statue within.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1040 == History == === Legendary Origins === According to the "Shōfuku-ji Engi," the temple was founded during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era (749–757), Gyōki received a divine oracle at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, instructing him to build a temple at "Tenrō Mine" (青峯), promising boundless spiritual benefits.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1039 In 806 CE, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}} returned from Tang China and, in 807 CE, under imperial edict, converted the temple to the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗}}. === Decline and Revival === After periods of decline, the temple was restored during the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享年間}} by the monk Ben’i. The belief that "pilgrimage to Aonomine protects against storms" spread, leading to a revival supported by the maritime trade of the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Shōfuku-ji reached its peak during the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1831), supported by merchants and seafarers from regions like Owari, Osaka, Ise, and Toba. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1125 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|ja|ジャスティン・トルドー}} visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|ja|第42回先進国首脳会議}} in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either {{ill|Matsuo Station|ja|松尾駅}} or {{ill|Kutsukake Station|ja|沓掛駅}}, both served by {{ill|Kintetsu Shima Line|ja|近鉄志摩線}}. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate.
