{{infobox religious building | name = Shōfuku-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Shouhukuji (Toba,Mie) 02.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall | location = {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} Matsuo-cho 519 | LatitudeDegrees = 34 | LatitudeMinutes = 24 | LatitudeSeconds = 37.4 | LongitudeDegrees = 136 | LongitudeMinutes = 49 | LongitudeSeconds = 35.5 | Map = Japan#Japan Mie | Mountainname = Aonomine-san | Sect = {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗}} | Subsect = Kōyasan Shingon | TempleRank = | Principaldeity = {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} | Yearconsecration_year = During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era | Founder = {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}} (traditionally attributed) | Restorer = During the Jōkyō era | Alias = Aonomine-san, Saga Palace | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|伊勢西国三十三所観音霊場}} (Non-numbered Temple)
{{ill|Tōkai-Kinki Jizō Pilgrimage|ja|東海近畿地蔵霊場}} (No. 17)
{{ill|Tōkai 36 Fudō Pilgrimage|ja|東海三十六不動尊霊場}} (No. 23)
{{ill|Mie Shikoku 88 Temples|ja|三重四国八十八箇所}} (No. 88) | CulturalProperty = Two Toba City-designated cultural properties | OfficialWebsite = }} '''Shōfuku-ji''' (正福寺) is a Buddhist temple of the {{ill|Kōyasan Shingon|ja|高野山真言宗}} sect, located in {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. Its mountain name is Aonomine-san (青峯山), situated near the summit of a 336-meter-high mountain of the same name.{{Cite web|url=https://isekannon.jp/wp-content/themes/isesaigoku2020/files/images/common/panf.pdf|title=Ise Saigoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|publisher=Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage Office|access-date=December 17, 2024}} It is also known by the alias "Saga Palace." Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 703 The temple is a sacred site for maritime safety, attracting fervent devotion from those connected to the fishing industry.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 818 Worshippers range from {{ill|Obira Town|ja|小平町}}, Hokkaido, to {{ill|Kagoshima City|ja|鹿児島市}}, Kagoshima.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 706 == Overview == Shōfuku-ji is located in a valley slightly below the peak of Aonomine-san (known as Tenato-yama) at the border of {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}} and {{ill|Shima City|ja|志摩市}}. Commonly referred to as "Aonomine-san," it is a rare {{ill|Shingon Buddhist temple|ja|真言宗}} in the {{ill|Shima Peninsula|ja|志摩地方}}, an area dominated by {{ill|Sōtō Zen|ja|曹洞宗}} temples.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 999 === Grounds === Within the mountain gate (constructed over 30 years, according to legend), there are several buildings, including the Main Hall (Kondō), Shōten Hall, Daishi Hall, Benzaiten Hall, Nyoirin Hall, Guest Hall, and the kitchen area.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1041 Visitors are allowed to ring the temple bell freely.Kinki Nippon Tourist Publishing Center (2005): p. 103 The Main Hall was built in 1836.[http://www.takenaka.co.jp/majorworks/30050qq1836-01.html?keepThis=true&TB_iframe=true (Takenaka Corporation)] === Principal deity === The temple enshrines the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} as its principal deity. The statue is revealed during the {{ill|Ofune Matsuri|ja|御船祭|Ofune Matsuri=Ofune Matsuri|lt=Ofune Festival}}. According to legend, the Kannon statue was a golden Buddha that appeared on the back of a whale from the sea off {{ill|Ousatsu|ja|相差村}} (present-day {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, Osatsu). The site where the whale surfaced is known as "Whale Cape," and the whale itself is said to have turned to stone, now referred to as "Whale Stone."{{Cite web|url=http://www.mietv.com/yajikita09/archive/090408/04.html|title=Whale Cape Promenade|publisher=Mie TV|access-date=May 15, 20qq}} The statue was later visited by a high-ranking monk known as Baramon Sōjō, who carved a wooden image to enshrine the luminous statue within.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1040 == History == === Legendary Origins === According to the "Shōfuku-ji Engi," the temple was founded during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era (749–757), Gyōki received a divine oracle at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, instructing him to build a temple at "Tenrō Mine" (青峯), promising boundless spiritual benefits.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1039 In 806 CE, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}} returned from Tang China and, in 807 CE, under imperial edict, converted the temple to the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗}}. === Decline and Revival === After periods of decline, the temple was restored during the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享年間}} by the monk Ben’i. The belief that "pilgrimage to Aonomine protects against storms" spread, leading to a revival supported by the maritime trade of the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Shōfuku-ji reached its peak during the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1831), supported by merchants and seafarers from regions like Owari, Osaka, Ise, and Toba. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. qq25 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|ja|ジャスティン・トルドー}} visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|ja|第42回先進国首脳会議}} in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either {{ill|Matsuo Station|ja|松尾駅}} or {{ill|Kutsukake Station|ja|沓掛駅}}, both served by {{ill|Kintetsu Shima Line|ja|近鉄志摩線}}. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. Main Hall | location = {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} Matsuo-cho 519 | LatitudeDegrees = 34 | LatitudeMinutes = 24 | LatitudeSeconds = 37.4 | LongitudeDegrees = 136 | LongitudeMinutes = 49 | LongitudeSeconds = 35.5 | Map = Japan#Japan Mie | Mountainname = Aonomine-san | Sect = {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗}} | Subsect = Kōyasan Shingon | TempleRank = | Principaldeity = {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} | Yearconsecration_year = During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era | Founder = {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}} (traditionally attributed) | Restorer = During the Jōkyō era | Alias = Aonomine-san, Saga Palace | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|伊勢西国三十三所観音霊場}} (Non-numbered Temple)
{{ill|Tōkai-Kinki Jizō Pilgrimage|ja|東海近畿地蔵霊場}} (No. 17)
{{ill|Tōkai 36 Fudō Pilgrimage|ja|東海三十六不動尊霊場}} (No. 23)
{{ill|Mie Shikoku 88 Temples|ja|三重四国八十八箇所}} (No. 88) | CulturalProperty = Two Toba City-designated cultural properties | OfficialWebsite = }} '''Shōfuku-ji''' (正福寺) is a Buddhist temple of the {{ill|Kōyasan Shingon|ja|高野山真言宗}} sect, located in {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. Its mountain name is Aonomine-san (青峯山), situated near the summit of a 336-meter-high mountain of the same name.{{Cite web|url=https://isekannon.jp/wp-content/themes/isesaigoku2020/files/images/common/panf.pdf|title=Ise Saigoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|publisher=Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage Office|access-date=December 17, 2024}} It is also known by the alias "Saga Palace." Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 703 The temple is a sacred site for maritime safety, attracting fervent devotion from those connected to the fishing industry.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 818 Worshippers range from {{ill|Obira Town|ja|小平町}}, Hokkaido, to {{ill|Kagoshima City|ja|鹿児島市}}, Kagoshima.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 706 == Overview == Shōfuku-ji is located in a valley slightly below the peak of Aonomine-san (known as Tenato-yama) at the border of {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}} and {{ill|Shima City|ja|志摩市}}. Commonly referred to as "Aonomine-san," it is a rare {{ill|Shingon Buddhist temple|ja|真言宗}} in the {{ill|Shima Peninsula|ja|志摩地方}}, an area dominated by {{ill|Sōtō Zen|ja|曹洞宗}} temples.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 999 === Grounds === Within the mountain gate (constructed over 30 years, according to legend), there are several buildings, including the Main Hall (Kondō), Shōten Hall, Daishi Hall, Benzaiten Hall, Nyoirin Hall, Guest Hall, and the kitchen area.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1041 Visitors are allowed to ring the temple bell freely.Kinki Nippon Tourist Publishing Center (2005): p. 103 The Main Hall was built in 1836.[http://www.takenaka.co.jp/majorworks/30050qq1836-01.html?keepThis=true&TB_iframe=true (Takenaka Corporation)] === Principal deity === The temple enshrines the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} as its principal deity. The statue is revealed during the {{ill|Ofune Matsuri|ja|御船祭|Ofune Matsuri=Ofune Matsuri|lt=Ofune Festival}}. According to legend, the Kannon statue was a golden Buddha that appeared on the back of a whale from the sea off {{ill|Ousatsu|ja|相差村}} (present-day {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, Osatsu). The site where the whale surfaced is known as "Whale Cape," and the whale itself is said to have turned to stone, now referred to as "Whale Stone."{{Cite web|url=http://www.mietv.com/yajikita09/archive/090408/04.html|title=Whale Cape Promenade|publisher=Mie TV|access-date=May 15, 20qq}} The statue was later visited by a high-ranking monk known as Baramon Sōjō, who carved a wooden image to enshrine the luminous statue within.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1040 == History == === Legendary Origins === According to the "Shōfuku-ji Engi," the temple was founded during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era (749–757), Gyōki received a divine oracle at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, instructing him to build a temple at "Tenrō Mine" (青峯), promising boundless spiritual benefits.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1039 In 806 CE, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}} returned from Tang China and, in 807 CE, under imperial edict, converted the temple to the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗}}. === Decline and Revival === After periods of decline, the temple was restored during the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享年間}} by the monk Ben’i. The belief that "pilgrimage to Aonomine protects against storms" spread, leading to a revival supported by the maritime trade of the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Shōfuku-ji reached its peak during the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1831), supported by merchants and seafarers from regions like Owari, Osaka, Ise, and Toba. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. qq25 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|ja|ジャスティン・トルドー}} visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|ja|第42回先進国首脳会議}} in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either {{ill|Matsuo Station|ja|松尾駅}} or {{ill|Kutsukake Station|ja|沓掛駅}}, both served by {{ill|Kintetsu Shima Line|ja|近鉄志摩線}}. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. Its mountain name is Aonomine-san (青峯山), situated near the summit of a 336-meter-high mountain of the same name.{{Cite web|url=https://isekannon.jp/wp-content/themes/isesaigoku2020/files/images/common/panf.pdf|title=Ise Saigoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|publisher=Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage Office|access-date=December 17, 2024}} It is also known by the alias "Saga Palace." Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 703 The temple is a sacred site for maritime safety, attracting fervent devotion from those connected to the fishing industry.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 818 Worshippers range from {{ill|Obira Town|ja|小平町}}, Hokkaido, to {{ill|Kagoshima City|ja|鹿児島市}}, Kagoshima.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 706 == Overview == Shōfuku-ji is located in a valley slightly below the peak of Aonomine-san (known as Tenato-yama) at the border of {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}} and {{ill|Shima City|ja|志摩市}}. Commonly referred to as "Aonomine-san," it is a rare {{ill|Shingon Buddhist temple|ja|真言宗}} in the {{ill|Shima Peninsula|ja|志摩地方}}, an area dominated by {{ill|Sōtō Zen|ja|曹洞宗}} temples.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 999 === Grounds === Within the mountain gate (constructed over 30 years, according to legend), there are several buildings, including the Main Hall (Kondō), Shōten Hall, Daishi Hall, Benzaiten Hall, Nyoirin Hall, Guest Hall, and the kitchen area.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1041 Visitors are allowed to ring the temple bell freely.Kinki Nippon Tourist Publishing Center (2005): p. 103 The Main Hall was built in 1836.[http://www.takenaka.co.jp/majorworks/30050qq1836-01.html?keepThis=true&TB_iframe=true (Takenaka Corporation)] === Principal deity === The temple enshrines the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} as its principal deity. The statue is revealed during the {{ill|Ofune Matsuri|ja|御船祭|Ofune Matsuri=Ofune Matsuri|lt=Ofune Festival}}. According to legend, the Kannon statue was a golden Buddha that appeared on the back of a whale from the sea off {{ill|Ousatsu|ja|相差村}} (present-day {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, Osatsu). The site where the whale surfaced is known as "Whale Cape," and the whale itself is said to have turned to stone, now referred to as "Whale Stone."{{Cite web|url=http://www.mietv.com/yajikita09/archive/090408/04.html|title=Whale Cape Promenade|publisher=Mie TV|access-date=May 15, 20qq}} The statue was later visited by a high-ranking monk known as Baramon Sōjō, who carved a wooden image to enshrine the luminous statue within.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1040 == History == === Legendary Origins === According to the "Shōfuku-ji Engi," the temple was founded during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era (749–757), Gyōki received a divine oracle at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, instructing him to build a temple at "Tenrō Mine" (青峯), promising boundless spiritual benefits.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1039 In 806 CE, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}} returned from Tang China and, in 807 CE, under imperial edict, converted the temple to the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗}}. === Decline and Revival === After periods of decline, the temple was restored during the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享年間}} by the monk Ben’i. The belief that "pilgrimage to Aonomine protects against storms" spread, leading to a revival supported by the maritime trade of the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Shōfuku-ji reached its peak during the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1831), supported by merchants and seafarers from regions like Owari, Osaka, Ise, and Toba. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. qq25 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|ja|ジャスティン・トルドー}} visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|ja|第42回先進国首脳会議}} in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either {{ill|Matsuo Station|ja|松尾駅}} or {{ill|Kutsukake Station|ja|沓掛駅}}, both served by {{ill|Kintetsu Shima Line|ja|近鉄志摩線}}. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. According to legend, the Kannon statue was a golden Buddha that appeared on the back of a whale from the sea off {{ill|Ousatsu|ja|相差村}} (present-day {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, Osatsu). The site where the whale surfaced is known as "Whale Cape," and the whale itself is said to have turned to stone, now referred to as "Whale Stone."{{Cite web|url=http://www.mietv.com/yajikita09/archive/090408/04.html|title=Whale Cape Promenade|publisher=Mie TV|access-date=May 15, 20qq}} The statue was later visited by a high-ranking monk known as Baramon Sōjō, who carved a wooden image to enshrine the luminous statue within.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1040 == History == === Legendary Origins === According to the "Shōfuku-ji Engi," the temple was founded during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era (749–757), Gyōki received a divine oracle at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, instructing him to build a temple at "Tenrō Mine" (青峯), promising boundless spiritual benefits.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1039 In 806 CE, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}} returned from Tang China and, in 807 CE, under imperial edict, converted the temple to the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗}}. === Decline and Revival === After periods of decline, the temple was restored during the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享年間}} by the monk Ben’i. The belief that "pilgrimage to Aonomine protects against storms" spread, leading to a revival supported by the maritime trade of the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Shōfuku-ji reached its peak during the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1831), supported by merchants and seafarers from regions like Owari, Osaka, Ise, and Toba. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. qq25 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|ja|ジャスティン・トルドー}} visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|ja|第42回先進国首脳会議}} in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either {{ill|Matsuo Station|ja|松尾駅}} or {{ill|Kutsukake Station|ja|沓掛駅}}, both served by {{ill|Kintetsu Shima Line|ja|近鉄志摩線}}. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. {{infobox religious building | name = Shōfuku-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Shouhukuji (Toba,Mie) 02.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall | location = {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} Matsuo-cho 519 | LatitudeDegrees = 34 | LatitudeMinutes = 24 | LatitudeSeconds = 37.4 | LongitudeDegrees = 136 | LongitudeMinutes = 49 | LongitudeSeconds = 35.5 | Map = Japan#Japan Mie | Mountainname = Aonomine-san | Sect = {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗}} | Subsect = Kōyasan Shingon | TempleRank = | Principaldeity = {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} | Yearconsecration_year = During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era | Founder = {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}} (traditionally attributed) | Restorer = During the Jōkyō era | Alias = Aonomine-san, Saga Palace | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|伊勢西国三十三所観音霊場}} (Non-numbered Temple)
{{ill|Tōkai-Kinki Jizō Pilgrimage|ja|東海近畿地蔵霊場}} (No. 17)
{{ill|Tōkai 36 Fudō Pilgrimage|ja|東海三十六不動尊霊場}} (No. 23)
{{ill|Mie Shikoku 88 Temples|ja|三重四国八十八箇所}} (No. 88) | CulturalProperty = Two Toba City-designated cultural properties | OfficialWebsite = }} '''Shōfuku-ji''' (正福寺) is a Buddhist temple of the {{ill|Kōyasan Shingon|ja|高野山真言宗}} sect, located in {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. Its mountain name is Aonomine-san (青峯山), situated near the summit of a 336-meter-high mountain of the same name.{{Cite web|url=https://isekannon.jp/wp-content/themes/isesaigoku2020/files/images/common/panf.pdf|title=Ise Saigoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|publisher=Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage Office|access-date=December 17, 2024}} It is also known by the alias "Saga Palace." Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 703 The temple is a sacred site for maritime safety, attracting fervent devotion from those connected to the fishing industry.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 818 Worshippers range from {{ill|Obira Town|ja|小平町}}, Hokkaido, to {{ill|Kagoshima City|ja|鹿児島市}}, Kagoshima.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 706 == Overview == Shōfuku-ji is located in a valley slightly below the peak of Aonomine-san (known as Tenato-yama) at the border of {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}} and {{ill|Shima City|ja|志摩市}}. Commonly referred to as "Aonomine-san," it is a rare {{ill|Shingon Buddhist temple|ja|真言宗}} in the {{ill|Shima Peninsula|ja|志摩地方}}, an area dominated by {{ill|Sōtō Zen|ja|曹洞宗}} temples.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 999 === Grounds === Within the mountain gate (constructed over 30 years, according to legend), there are several buildings, including the Main Hall (Kondō), Shōten Hall, Daishi Hall, Benzaiten Hall, Nyoirin Hall, Guest Hall, and the kitchen area.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1041 Visitors are allowed to ring the temple bell freely.Kinki Nippon Tourist Publishing Center (2005): p. 103 The Main Hall was built in 1836.[http://www.takenaka.co.jp/majorworks/30050qq1836-01.html?keepThis=true&TB_iframe=true (Takenaka Corporation)] === Principal deity === The temple enshrines the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} as its principal deity. The statue is revealed during the {{ill|Ofune Matsuri|ja|御船祭|Ofune Matsuri=Ofune Matsuri|lt=Ofune Festival}}. According to legend, the Kannon statue was a golden Buddha that appeared on the back of a whale from the sea off {{ill|Ousatsu|ja|相差村}} (present-day {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, Osatsu). The site where the whale surfaced is known as "Whale Cape," and the whale itself is said to have turned to stone, now referred to as "Whale Stone."{{Cite web|url=http://www.mietv.com/yajikita09/archive/090408/04.html|title=Whale Cape Promenade|publisher=Mie TV|access-date=May 15, 20qq}} The statue was later visited by a high-ranking monk known as Baramon Sōjō, who carved a wooden image to enshrine the luminous statue within.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1040 == History == === Legendary Origins === According to the "Shōfuku-ji Engi," the temple was founded during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era (749–757), Gyōki received a divine oracle at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, instructing him to build a temple at "Tenrō Mine" (青峯), promising boundless spiritual benefits.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1039 In 806 CE, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}} returned from Tang China and, in 807 CE, under imperial edict, converted the temple to the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗}}. === Decline and Revival === After periods of decline, the temple was restored during the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享年間}} by the monk Ben’i. The belief that "pilgrimage to Aonomine protects against storms" spread, leading to a revival supported by the maritime trade of the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Shōfuku-ji reached its peak during the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1831), supported by merchants and seafarers from regions like Owari, Osaka, Ise, and Toba. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. qq25 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|ja|ジャスティン・トルドー}} visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|ja|第42回先進国首脳会議}} in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either {{ill|Matsuo Station|ja|松尾駅}} or {{ill|Kutsukake Station|ja|沓掛駅}}, both served by {{ill|Kintetsu Shima Line|ja|近鉄志摩線}}. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. {{infobox religious building | name = Shōfuku-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Shouhukuji (Toba,Mie) 02.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall | location = {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} Matsuo-cho 519 | LatitudeDegrees = 34 | LatitudeMinutes = 24 | LatitudeSeconds = 37.4 | LongitudeDegrees = 136 | LongitudeMinutes = 49 | LongitudeSeconds = 35.5 | Map = Japan#Japan Mie | Mountainname = Aonomine-san | Sect = {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗}} | Subsect = Kōyasan Shingon | TempleRank = | Principaldeity = {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} | Yearconsecration_year = During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era | Founder = {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}} (traditionally attributed) | Restorer = During the Jōkyō era | Alias = Aonomine-san, Saga Palace | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|伊勢西国三十三所観音霊場}} (Non-numbered Temple)
{{ill|Tōkai-Kinki Jizō Pilgrimage|ja|東海近畿地蔵霊場}} (No. 17)
{{ill|Tōkai 36 Fudō Pilgrimage|ja|東海三十六不動尊霊場}} (No. 23)
{{ill|Mie Shikoku 88 Temples|ja|三重四国八十八箇所}} (No. 88) | CulturalProperty = Two Toba City-designated cultural properties | OfficialWebsite = }} '''Shōfuku-ji''' (正福寺) is a Buddhist temple of the {{ill|Kōyasan Shingon|ja|高野山真言宗}} sect, located in {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. Its mountain name is Aonomine-san (青峯山), situated near the summit of a 336-meter-high mountain of the same name.{{Cite web|url=https://isekannon.jp/wp-content/themes/isesaigoku2020/files/images/common/panf.pdf|title=Ise Saigoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|publisher=Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage Office|access-date=December 17, 2024}} It is also known by the alias "Saga Palace." Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 703 The temple is a sacred site for maritime safety, attracting fervent devotion from those connected to the fishing industry.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 818 Worshippers range from {{ill|Obira Town|ja|小平町}}, Hokkaido, to {{ill|Kagoshima City|ja|鹿児島市}}, Kagoshima.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 706 == Overview == Shōfuku-ji is located in a valley slightly below the peak of Aonomine-san (known as Tenato-yama) at the border of {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}} and {{ill|Shima City|ja|志摩市}}. Commonly referred to as "Aonomine-san," it is a rare {{ill|Shingon Buddhist temple|ja|真言宗}} in the {{ill|Shima Peninsula|ja|志摩地方}}, an area dominated by {{ill|Sōtō Zen|ja|曹洞宗}} temples.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 999 === Grounds === Within the mountain gate (constructed over 30 years, according to legend), there are several buildings, including the Main Hall (Kondō), Shōten Hall, Daishi Hall, Benzaiten Hall, Nyoirin Hall, Guest Hall, and the kitchen area.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1041 Visitors are allowed to ring the temple bell freely.Kinki Nippon Tourist Publishing Center (2005): p. 103 The Main Hall was built in 1836.[http://www.takenaka.co.jp/majorworks/30050qq1836-01.html?keepThis=true&TB_iframe=true (Takenaka Corporation)] === Principal deity === The temple enshrines the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} as its principal deity. The statue is revealed during the {{ill|Ofune Matsuri|ja|御船祭|Ofune Matsuri=Ofune Matsuri|lt=Ofune Festival}}. According to legend, the Kannon statue was a golden Buddha that appeared on the back of a whale from the sea off {{ill|Ousatsu|ja|相差村}} (present-day {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, Osatsu). The site where the whale surfaced is known as "Whale Cape," and the whale itself is said to have turned to stone, now referred to as "Whale Stone."{{Cite web|url=http://www.mietv.com/yajikita09/archive/090408/04.html|title=Whale Cape Promenade|publisher=Mie TV|access-date=May 15, 20qq}} The statue was later visited by a high-ranking monk known as Baramon Sōjō, who carved a wooden image to enshrine the luminous statue within.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1040 == History == === Legendary Origins === According to the "Shōfuku-ji Engi," the temple was founded during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era (749–757), Gyōki received a divine oracle at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, instructing him to build a temple at "Tenrō Mine" (青峯), promising boundless spiritual benefits.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1039 In 806 CE, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}} returned from Tang China and, in 807 CE, under imperial edict, converted the temple to the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗}}. === Decline and Revival === After periods of decline, the temple was restored during the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享年間}} by the monk Ben’i. The belief that "pilgrimage to Aonomine protects against storms" spread, leading to a revival supported by the maritime trade of the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Shōfuku-ji reached its peak during the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1831), supported by merchants and seafarers from regions like Owari, Osaka, Ise, and Toba. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. qq25 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|ja|ジャスティン・トルドー}} visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|ja|第42回先進国首脳会議}} in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either {{ill|Matsuo Station|ja|松尾駅}} or {{ill|Kutsukake Station|ja|沓掛駅}}, both served by {{ill|Kintetsu Shima Line|ja|近鉄志摩線}}. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. {{infobox religious building | name = Shōfuku-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Shouhukuji (Toba,Mie) 02.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall | location = {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} Matsuo-cho 519 | LatitudeDegrees = 34 | LatitudeMinutes = 24 | LatitudeSeconds = 37.4 | LongitudeDegrees = 136 | LongitudeMinutes = 49 | LongitudeSeconds = 35.5 | Map = Japan#Japan Mie | Mountainname = Aonomine-san | Sect = {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗}} | Subsect = Kōyasan Shingon | TempleRank = | Principaldeity = {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} | Yearconsecration_year = During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era | Founder = {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}} (traditionally attributed) | Restorer = During the Jōkyō era | Alias = Aonomine-san, Saga Palace | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|伊勢西国三十三所観音霊場}} (Non-numbered Temple)
{{ill|Tōkai-Kinki Jizō Pilgrimage|ja|東海近畿地蔵霊場}} (No. 17)
{{ill|Tōkai 36 Fudō Pilgrimage|ja|東海三十六不動尊霊場}} (No. 23)
{{ill|Mie Shikoku 88 Temples|ja|三重四国八十八箇所}} (No. 88) | CulturalProperty = Two Toba City-designated cultural properties | OfficialWebsite = }} '''Shōfuku-ji''' (正福寺) is a Buddhist temple of the {{ill|Kōyasan Shingon|ja|高野山真言宗}} sect, located in {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. Its mountain name is Aonomine-san (青峯山), situated near the summit of a 336-meter-high mountain of the same name.{{Cite web|url=https://isekannon.jp/wp-content/themes/isesaigoku2020/files/images/common/panf.pdf|title=Ise Saigoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|publisher=Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage Office|access-date=December 17, 2024}} It is also known by the alias "Saga Palace." Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 703 The temple is a sacred site for maritime safety, attracting fervent devotion from those connected to the fishing industry.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 818 Worshippers range from {{ill|Obira Town|ja|小平町}}, Hokkaido, to {{ill|Kagoshima City|ja|鹿児島市}}, Kagoshima.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 706 == Overview == Shōfuku-ji is located in a valley slightly below the peak of Aonomine-san (known as Tenato-yama) at the border of {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}} and {{ill|Shima City|ja|志摩市}}. Commonly referred to as "Aonomine-san," it is a rare {{ill|Shingon Buddhist temple|ja|真言宗}} in the {{ill|Shima Peninsula|ja|志摩地方}}, an area dominated by {{ill|Sōtō Zen|ja|曹洞宗}} temples.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 999 === Grounds === Within the mountain gate (constructed over 30 years, according to legend), there are several buildings, including the Main Hall (Kondō), Shōten Hall, Daishi Hall, Benzaiten Hall, Nyoirin Hall, Guest Hall, and the kitchen area.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1041 Visitors are allowed to ring the temple bell freely.Kinki Nippon Tourist Publishing Center (2005): p. 103 The Main Hall was built in 1836.[http://www.takenaka.co.jp/majorworks/30050qq1836-01.html?keepThis=true&TB_iframe=true (Takenaka Corporation)] === Principal deity === The temple enshrines the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} as its principal deity. The statue is revealed during the {{ill|Ofune Matsuri|ja|御船祭|Ofune Matsuri=Ofune Matsuri|lt=Ofune Festival}}. According to legend, the Kannon statue was a golden Buddha that appeared on the back of a whale from the sea off {{ill|Ousatsu|ja|相差村}} (present-day {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, Osatsu). The site where the whale surfaced is known as "Whale Cape," and the whale itself is said to have turned to stone, now referred to as "Whale Stone."{{Cite web|url=http://www.mietv.com/yajikita09/archive/090408/04.html|title=Whale Cape Promenade|publisher=Mie TV|access-date=May 15, 20qq}} The statue was later visited by a high-ranking monk known as Baramon Sōjō, who carved a wooden image to enshrine the luminous statue within.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1040 == History == === Legendary Origins === According to the "Shōfuku-ji Engi," the temple was founded during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era (749–757), Gyōki received a divine oracle at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, instructing him to build a temple at "Tenrō Mine" (青峯), promising boundless spiritual benefits.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1039 In 806 CE, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}} returned from Tang China and, in 807 CE, under imperial edict, converted the temple to the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗}}. === Decline and Revival === After periods of decline, the temple was restored during the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享年間}} by the monk Ben’i. The belief that "pilgrimage to Aonomine protects against storms" spread, leading to a revival supported by the maritime trade of the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Shōfuku-ji reached its peak during the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1831), supported by merchants and seafarers from regions like Owari, Osaka, Ise, and Toba. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. qq25 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|ja|ジャスティン・トルドー}} visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|ja|第42回先進国首脳会議}} in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either {{ill|Matsuo Station|ja|松尾駅}} or {{ill|Kutsukake Station|ja|沓掛駅}}, both served by {{ill|Kintetsu Shima Line|ja|近鉄志摩線}}. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. {{ill|Tōkai-Kinki Jizō Pilgrimage|ja|東海近畿地蔵霊場}} (No. 17)
{{ill|Tōkai 36 Fudō Pilgrimage|ja|東海三十六不動尊霊場}} (No. 23)
{{ill|Mie Shikoku 88 Temples|ja|三重四国八十八箇所}} (No. 88) | CulturalProperty = Two Toba City-designated cultural properties | OfficialWebsite = }} '''Shōfuku-ji''' (正福寺) is a Buddhist temple of the {{ill|Kōyasan Shingon|ja|高野山真言宗}} sect, located in {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. Its mountain name is Aonomine-san (青峯山), situated near the summit of a 336-meter-high mountain of the same name.{{Cite web|url=https://isekannon.jp/wp-content/themes/isesaigoku2020/files/images/common/panf.pdf|title=Ise Saigoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|publisher=Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage Office|access-date=December 17, 2024}} It is also known by the alias "Saga Palace." Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 703 The temple is a sacred site for maritime safety, attracting fervent devotion from those connected to the fishing industry.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 818 Worshippers range from {{ill|Obira Town|ja|小平町}}, Hokkaido, to {{ill|Kagoshima City|ja|鹿児島市}}, Kagoshima.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 706 == Overview == Shōfuku-ji is located in a valley slightly below the peak of Aonomine-san (known as Tenato-yama) at the border of {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}} and {{ill|Shima City|ja|志摩市}}. Commonly referred to as "Aonomine-san," it is a rare {{ill|Shingon Buddhist temple|ja|真言宗}} in the {{ill|Shima Peninsula|ja|志摩地方}}, an area dominated by {{ill|Sōtō Zen|ja|曹洞宗}} temples.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 999 === Grounds === Within the mountain gate (constructed over 30 years, according to legend), there are several buildings, including the Main Hall (Kondō), Shōten Hall, Daishi Hall, Benzaiten Hall, Nyoirin Hall, Guest Hall, and the kitchen area.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1041 Visitors are allowed to ring the temple bell freely.Kinki Nippon Tourist Publishing Center (2005): p. 103 The Main Hall was built in 1836.[http://www.takenaka.co.jp/majorworks/30050111836-01.html?keepThis=true&TB_iframe=true (Takenaka Corporation)] === Principal deity === The temple enshrines the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} as its principal deity. The statue is revealed during the {{ill|Ofune Matsuri|ja|御船祭|Ofune Matsuri=Ofune Matsuri|lt=Ofune Festival}}. According to legend, the Kannon statue was a golden Buddha that appeared on the back of a whale from the sea off {{ill|Ousatsu|ja|相差村}} (present-day {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, Osatsu). The site where the whale surfaced is known as "Whale Cape," and the whale itself is said to have turned to stone, now referred to as "Whale Stone."{{Cite web|url=http://www.mietv.com/yajikita09/archive/090408/04.html|title=Whale Cape Promenade|publisher=Mie TV|access-date=May 15, 2011}} The statue was later visited by a high-ranking monk known as Baramon Sōjō, who carved a wooden image to enshrine the luminous statue within.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1040 == History == === Legendary Origins === According to the "Shōfuku-ji Engi," the temple was founded during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era (749–757), Gyōki received a divine oracle at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, instructing him to build a temple at "Tenrō Mine" (青峯), promising boundless spiritual benefits.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1039 In 806 CE, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}} returned from Tang China and, in 807 CE, under imperial edict, converted the temple to the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗}}. === Decline and Revival === After periods of decline, the temple was restored during the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享年間}} by the monk Ben’i. The belief that "pilgrimage to Aonomine protects against storms" spread, leading to a revival supported by the maritime trade of the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Shōfuku-ji reached its peak during the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1831), supported by merchants and seafarers from regions like Owari, Osaka, Ise, and Toba. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1125 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|ja|ジャスティン・トルドー}} visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|ja|第42回先進国首脳会議}} in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either {{ill|Matsuo Station|ja|松尾駅}} or {{ill|Kutsukake Station|ja|沓掛駅}}, both served by {{ill|Kintetsu Shima Line|ja|近鉄志摩線}}. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. Its mountain name is Aonomine-san (青峯山), situated near the summit of a 336-meter-high mountain of the same name.{{Cite web|url=https://isekannon.jp/wp-content/themes/isesaigoku2020/files/images/common/panf.pdf|title=Ise Saigoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|publisher=Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage Office|access-date=December 17, 2024}} It is also known by the alias "Saga Palace." Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 703 The temple is a sacred site for maritime safety, attracting fervent devotion from those connected to the fishing industry.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 818 Worshippers range from {{ill|Obira Town|ja|小平町}}, Hokkaido, to {{ill|Kagoshima City|ja|鹿児島市}}, Kagoshima.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 706 == Overview == Shōfuku-ji is located in a valley slightly below the peak of Aonomine-san (known as Tenato-yama) at the border of {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}} and {{ill|Shima City|ja|志摩市}}. Commonly referred to as "Aonomine-san," it is a rare {{ill|Shingon Buddhist temple|ja|真言宗}} in the {{ill|Shima Peninsula|ja|志摩地方}}, an area dominated by {{ill|Sōtō Zen|ja|曹洞宗}} temples.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 999 === Grounds === Within the mountain gate (constructed over 30 years, according to legend), there are several buildings, including the Main Hall (Kondō), Shōten Hall, Daishi Hall, Benzaiten Hall, Nyoirin Hall, Guest Hall, and the kitchen area.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1041 Visitors are allowed to ring the temple bell freely.Kinki Nippon Tourist Publishing Center (2005): p. 103 The Main Hall was built in 1836.[http://www.takenaka.co.jp/majorworks/30050111836-01.html?keepThis=true&TB_iframe=true (Takenaka Corporation)] === Principal deity === The temple enshrines the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} as its principal deity. The statue is revealed during the {{ill|Ofune Matsuri|ja|御船祭|Ofune Matsuri=Ofune Matsuri|lt=Ofune Festival}}. According to legend, the Kannon statue was a golden Buddha that appeared on the back of a whale from the sea off {{ill|Ousatsu|ja|相差村}} (present-day {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, Osatsu). The site where the whale surfaced is known as "Whale Cape," and the whale itself is said to have turned to stone, now referred to as "Whale Stone."{{Cite web|url=http://www.mietv.com/yajikita09/archive/090408/04.html|title=Whale Cape Promenade|publisher=Mie TV|access-date=May 15, 2011}} The statue was later visited by a high-ranking monk known as Baramon Sōjō, who carved a wooden image to enshrine the luminous statue within.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1040 == History == === Legendary Origins === According to the "Shōfuku-ji Engi," the temple was founded during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era (749–757), Gyōki received a divine oracle at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, instructing him to build a temple at "Tenrō Mine" (青峯), promising boundless spiritual benefits.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1039 In 806 CE, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}} returned from Tang China and, in 807 CE, under imperial edict, converted the temple to the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗}}. === Decline and Revival === After periods of decline, the temple was restored during the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享年間}} by the monk Ben’i. The belief that "pilgrimage to Aonomine protects against storms" spread, leading to a revival supported by the maritime trade of the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Shōfuku-ji reached its peak during the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1831), supported by merchants and seafarers from regions like Owari, Osaka, Ise, and Toba. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1125 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|ja|ジャスティン・トルドー}} visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|ja|第42回先進国首脳会議}} in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either {{ill|Matsuo Station|ja|松尾駅}} or {{ill|Kutsukake Station|ja|沓掛駅}}, both served by {{ill|Kintetsu Shima Line|ja|近鉄志摩線}}. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. The site where the whale surfaced is known as "Whale Cape," and the whale itself is said to have turned to stone, now referred to as "Whale Stone."{{Cite web|url=http://www.mietv.com/yajikita09/archive/090408/04.html|title=Whale Cape Promenade|publisher=Mie TV|access-date=May 15, 2011}} The statue was later visited by a high-ranking monk known as Baramon Sōjō, who carved a wooden image to enshrine the luminous statue within.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1040 == History == === Legendary Origins === According to the "Shōfuku-ji Engi," the temple was founded during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era (749–757), Gyōki received a divine oracle at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, instructing him to build a temple at "Tenrō Mine" (青峯), promising boundless spiritual benefits.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1039 In 806 CE, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}} returned from Tang China and, in 807 CE, under imperial edict, converted the temple to the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗}}. === Decline and Revival === After periods of decline, the temple was restored during the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享年間}} by the monk Ben’i. The belief that "pilgrimage to Aonomine protects against storms" spread, leading to a revival supported by the maritime trade of the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Shōfuku-ji reached its peak during the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1831), supported by merchants and seafarers from regions like Owari, Osaka, Ise, and Toba. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1125 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|ja|ジャスティン・トルドー}} visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|ja|第42回先進国首脳会議}} in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either {{ill|Matsuo Station|ja|松尾駅}} or {{ill|Kutsukake Station|ja|沓掛駅}}, both served by {{ill|Kintetsu Shima Line|ja|近鉄志摩線}}. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. {{ill|Mie Shikoku 88 Temples|ja|三重四国八十八箇所}} (No. 88) | CulturalProperty = Two Toba City-designated cultural properties | OfficialWebsite = }} '''Shōfuku-ji''' (正福寺) is a Buddhist temple of the {{ill|Kōyasan Shingon|ja|高野山真言宗}} sect, located in {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. Its mountain name is Aonomine-san (青峯山), situated near the summit of a 336-meter-high mountain of the same name.{{Cite web|url=https://isekannon.jp/wp-content/themes/isesaigoku2020/files/images/common/panf.pdf|title=Ise Saigoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|publisher=Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage Office|access-date=December 17, 2024}} It is also known by the alias "Saga Palace." Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 703 The temple is a sacred site for maritime safety, attracting fervent devotion from those connected to the fishing industry.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 818 Worshippers range from {{ill|Obira Town|ja|小平町}}, Hokkaido, to {{ill|Kagoshima City|ja|鹿児島市}}, Kagoshima.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 706 == Overview == Shōfuku-ji is located in a valley slightly below the peak of Aonomine-san (known as Tenato-yama) at the border of {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}} and {{ill|Shima City|ja|志摩市}}. Commonly referred to as "Aonomine-san," it is a rare {{ill|Shingon Buddhist temple|ja|真言宗}} in the {{ill|Shima Peninsula|ja|志摩地方}}, an area dominated by {{ill|Sōtō Zen|ja|曹洞宗}} temples.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 999 === Grounds === Within the mountain gate (constructed over 30 years, according to legend), there are several buildings, including the Main Hall (Kondō), Shōten Hall, Daishi Hall, Benzaiten Hall, Nyoirin Hall, Guest Hall, and the kitchen area.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1041 Visitors are allowed to ring the temple bell freely.Kinki Nippon Tourist Publishing Center (2005): p. 103 The Main Hall was built in 1836.[http://www.takenaka.co.jp/majorworks/30050111836-01.html?keepThis=true&TB_iframe=true (Takenaka Corporation)] === Principal deity === The temple enshrines the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} as its principal deity. The statue is revealed during the {{ill|Ofune Matsuri|ja|御船祭|Ofune Matsuri=Ofune Matsuri|lt=Ofune Festival}}. According to legend, the Kannon statue was a golden Buddha that appeared on the back of a whale from the sea off {{ill|Ousatsu|ja|相差村}} (present-day {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, Osatsu). The site where the whale surfaced is known as "Whale Cape," and the whale itself is said to have turned to stone, now referred to as "Whale Stone."{{Cite web|url=http://www.mietv.com/yajikita09/archive/090408/04.html|title=Whale Cape Promenade|publisher=Mie TV|access-date=May 15, 2011}} The statue was later visited by a high-ranking monk known as Baramon Sōjō, who carved a wooden image to enshrine the luminous statue within.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1040 == History == === Legendary Origins === According to the "Shōfuku-ji Engi," the temple was founded during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era (749–757), Gyōki received a divine oracle at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, instructing him to build a temple at "Tenrō Mine" (青峯), promising boundless spiritual benefits.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1039 In 806 CE, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}} returned from Tang China and, in 807 CE, under imperial edict, converted the temple to the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗}}. === Decline and Revival === After periods of decline, the temple was restored during the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享年間}} by the monk Ben’i. The belief that "pilgrimage to Aonomine protects against storms" spread, leading to a revival supported by the maritime trade of the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Shōfuku-ji reached its peak during the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1831), supported by merchants and seafarers from regions like Owari, Osaka, Ise, and Toba. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1125 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|ja|ジャスティン・トルドー}} visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|ja|第42回先進国首脳会議}} in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either {{ill|Matsuo Station|ja|松尾駅}} or {{ill|Kutsukake Station|ja|沓掛駅}}, both served by {{ill|Kintetsu Shima Line|ja|近鉄志摩線}}. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1125 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|ja|ジャスティン・トルドー}} visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|ja|第42回先進国首脳会議}} in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either {{ill|Matsuo Station|ja|松尾駅}} or {{ill|Kutsukake Station|ja|沓掛駅}}, both served by {{ill|Kintetsu Shima Line|ja|近鉄志摩線}}. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. {{infobox religious building | name = Shōfuku-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Shouhukuji (Toba,Mie) 02.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall | location = {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} Matsuo-cho 519 | LatitudeDegrees = 34 | LatitudeMinutes = 24 | LatitudeSeconds = 37.4 | LongitudeDegrees = 136 | LongitudeMinutes = 49 | LongitudeSeconds = 35.5 | Map = Japan#Japan Mie | Mountainname = Aonomine-san | Sect = {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗}} | Subsect = Kōyasan Shingon | TempleRank = | Principaldeity = {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} | Yearconsecration_year = During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era | Founder = {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}} (traditionally attributed) | Restorer = During the Jōkyō era | Alias = Aonomine-san, Saga Palace | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|伊勢西国三十三所観音霊場}} (Non-numbered Temple)
{{ill|Tōkai-Kinki Jizō Pilgrimage|ja|東海近畿地蔵霊場}} (No. 17)
{{ill|Tōkai 36 Fudō Pilgrimage|ja|東海三十六不動尊霊場}} (No. 23)
{{ill|Mie Shikoku 88 Temples|ja|三重四国八十八箇所}} (No. 88) | CulturalProperty = Two Toba City-designated cultural properties | OfficialWebsite = }} '''Shōfuku-ji''' (正福寺) is a Buddhist temple of the {{ill|Kōyasan Shingon|ja|高野山真言宗}} sect, located in {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. Its mountain name is Aonomine-san (青峯山), situated near the summit of a 336-meter-high mountain of the same name.{{Cite web|url=https://isekannon.jp/wp-content/themes/isesaigoku2020/files/images/common/panf.pdf|title=Ise Saigoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|publisher=Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage Office|access-date=December 17, 2024}} It is also known by the alias "Saga Palace." Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 703 The temple is a sacred site for maritime safety, attracting fervent devotion from those connected to the fishing industry.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 818 Worshippers range from {{ill|Obira Town|ja|小平町}}, Hokkaido, to {{ill|Kagoshima City|ja|鹿児島市}}, Kagoshima.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 706 == Overview == Shōfuku-ji is located in a valley slightly below the peak of Aonomine-san (known as Tenato-yama) at the border of {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}} and {{ill|Shima City|ja|志摩市}}. Commonly referred to as "Aonomine-san," it is a rare {{ill|Shingon Buddhist temple|ja|真言宗}} in the {{ill|Shima Peninsula|ja|志摩地方}}, an area dominated by {{ill|Sōtō Zen|ja|曹洞宗}} temples.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 999 === Grounds === Within the mountain gate (constructed over 30 years, according to legend), there are several buildings, including the Main Hall (Kondō), Shōten Hall, Daishi Hall, Benzaiten Hall, Nyoirin Hall, Guest Hall, and the kitchen area.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1041 Visitors are allowed to ring the temple bell freely.Kinki Nippon Tourist Publishing Center (2005): p. 103 The Main Hall was built in 1836.[http://www.takenaka.co.jp/majorworks/30050111836-01.html?keepThis=true&TB_iframe=true (Takenaka Corporation)] === Principal deity === The temple enshrines the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} as its principal deity. The statue is revealed during the {{ill|Ofune Matsuri|ja|御船祭|Ofune Matsuri=Ofune Matsuri|lt=Ofune Festival}}. According to legend, the Kannon statue was a golden Buddha that appeared on the back of a whale from the sea off {{ill|Ousatsu|ja|相差村}} (present-day {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, Osatsu). The site where the whale surfaced is known as "Whale Cape," and the whale itself is said to have turned to stone, now referred to as "Whale Stone."{{Cite web|url=http://www.mietv.com/yajikita09/archive/090408/04.html|title=Whale Cape Promenade|publisher=Mie TV|access-date=May 15, 2011}} The statue was later visited by a high-ranking monk known as Baramon Sōjō, who carved a wooden image to enshrine the luminous statue within.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1040 == History == === Legendary Origins === According to the "Shōfuku-ji Engi," the temple was founded during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era (749–757), Gyōki received a divine oracle at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, instructing him to build a temple at "Tenrō Mine" (青峯), promising boundless spiritual benefits.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1039 In 806 CE, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}} returned from Tang China and, in 807 CE, under imperial edict, converted the temple to the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗}}. === Decline and Revival === After periods of decline, the temple was restored during the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享年間}} by the monk Ben’i. The belief that "pilgrimage to Aonomine protects against storms" spread, leading to a revival supported by the maritime trade of the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Shōfuku-ji reached its peak during the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1831), supported by merchants and seafarers from regions like Owari, Osaka, Ise, and Toba. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1125 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|ja|ジャスティン・トルドー}} visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|ja|第42回先進国首脳会議}} in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either {{ill|Matsuo Station|ja|松尾駅}} or {{ill|Kutsukake Station|ja|沓掛駅}}, both served by {{ill|Kintetsu Shima Line|ja|近鉄志摩線}}. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. revision.text:
{{infobox religious building | name = Shōfuku-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Shouhukuji (Toba,Mie) 02.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall | location = {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} Matsuo-cho 519 | LatitudeDegrees = 34 | LatitudeMinutes = 24 | LatitudeSeconds = 37.4 | LongitudeDegrees = 136 | LongitudeMinutes = 49 | LongitudeSeconds = 35.5 | Map = Japan#Japan Mie | Mountainname = Aonomine-san | Sect = {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗}} | Subsect = Kōyasan Shingon | TempleRank = | Principaldeity = {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} | Yearconsecration_year = During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era | Founder = {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}} (traditionally attributed) | Restorer = During the Jōkyō era | Alias = Aonomine-san, Saga Palace | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|伊勢西国三十三所観音霊場}} (Non-numbered Temple)
{{ill|Tōkai-Kinki Jizō Pilgrimage|ja|東海近畿地蔵霊場}} (No. 17)
{{ill|Tōkai 36 Fudō Pilgrimage|ja|東海三十六不動尊霊場}} (No. 23)
{{ill|Mie Shikoku 88 Temples|ja|三重四国八十八箇所}} (No. 88) | CulturalProperty = Two Toba City-designated cultural properties | OfficialWebsite = }} '''Shōfuku-ji''' (正福寺) is a Buddhist temple of the {{ill|Kōyasan Shingon|ja|高野山真言宗}} sect, located in {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. Its mountain name is Aonomine-san (青峯山), situated near the summit of a 336-meter-high mountain of the same name.{{Cite web|url=https://isekannon.jp/wp-content/themes/isesaigoku2020/files/images/common/panf.pdf|title=Ise Saigoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|publisher=Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage Office|access-date=December 17, 2024}} It is also known by the alias "Saga Palace." Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 703 The temple is a sacred site for maritime safety, attracting fervent devotion from those connected to the fishing industry.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 818 Worshippers range from {{ill|Obira Town|ja|小平町}}, Hokkaido, to {{ill|Kagoshima City|ja|鹿児島市}}, Kagoshima.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 706 == Overview == Shōfuku-ji is located in a valley slightly below the peak of Aonomine-san (known as Tenato-yama) at the border of {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}} and {{ill|Shima City|ja|志摩市}}. Commonly referred to as "Aonomine-san," it is a rare {{ill|Shingon Buddhist temple|ja|真言宗}} in the {{ill|Shima Peninsula|ja|志摩地方}}, an area dominated by {{ill|Sōtō Zen|ja|曹洞宗}} temples.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 999 === Grounds === Within the mountain gate (constructed over 30 years, according to legend), there are several buildings, including the Main Hall (Kondō), Shōten Hall, Daishi Hall, Benzaiten Hall, Nyoirin Hall, Guest Hall, and the kitchen area.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1041 Visitors are allowed to ring the temple bell freely.Kinki Nippon Tourist Publishing Center (2005): p. 103 The Main Hall was built in 1836.[http://www.takenaka.co.jp/majorworks/30050111836-01.html?keepThis=true&TB_iframe=true (Takenaka Corporation)] === Principal deity === The temple enshrines the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} as its principal deity. The statue is revealed during the {{ill|Ofune Matsuri|ja|御船祭|Ofune Matsuri=Ofune Matsuri|lt=Ofune Festival}}. According to legend, the Kannon statue was a golden Buddha that appeared on the back of a whale from the sea off {{ill|Ousatsu|ja|相差村}} (present-day {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市}}, Osatsu). The site where the whale surfaced is known as "Whale Cape," and the whale itself is said to have turned to stone, now referred to as "Whale Stone."{{Cite web|url=http://www.mietv.com/yajikita09/archive/090408/04.html|title=Whale Cape Promenade|publisher=Mie TV|access-date=May 15, 2011}} The statue was later visited by a high-ranking monk known as Baramon Sōjō, who carved a wooden image to enshrine the luminous statue within.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1040 == History == === Legendary Origins === According to the "Shōfuku-ji Engi," the temple was founded during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era (749–757), Gyōki received a divine oracle at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, instructing him to build a temple at "Tenrō Mine" (青峯), promising boundless spiritual benefits.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1039 In 806 CE, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}} returned from Tang China and, in 807 CE, under imperial edict, converted the temple to the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗}}. === Decline and Revival === After periods of decline, the temple was restored during the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享年間}} by the monk Ben’i. The belief that "pilgrimage to Aonomine protects against storms" spread, leading to a revival supported by the maritime trade of the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Shōfuku-ji reached its peak during the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1831), supported by merchants and seafarers from regions like Owari, Osaka, Ise, and Toba. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1125 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|ja|ジャスティン・トルドー}} visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|ja|第42回先進国首脳会議}} in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either {{ill|Matsuo Station|ja|松尾駅}} or {{ill|Kutsukake Station|ja|沓掛駅}}, both served by {{ill|Kintetsu Shima Line|ja|近鉄志摩線}}. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Shoufukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Shingon Buddhism]] [[Category:Cultural properties in Japan]] [[Category:Toba City]] [[Category:Mie Shikoku Pilgrimage]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Q11]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[sn:Shoufukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[ja:正福寺 (鳥羽市)]] [[sn:houfukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[en:Shoufukuji Temple (Toba City)]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shoufukuji Temple (Toba City)}}

According to legend, the Kannon statue was a golden Buddha that appeared on the back of a whale from the sea off {{ill|Ousatsu|ja|相差村|12=draft}} (present-day {{ill|Toba City|ja|鳥羽市|12=draft}}, Osatsu). During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era (749–757), Gyōki received a divine oracle at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}}, instructing him to build a temple at "Tenrō Mine" (青峯), promising boundless spiritual benefits.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1039 In 806 CE, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海|12=draft}} returned from Tang China and, in 807 CE, under imperial edict, converted the temple to the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗|12=draft}}. === Decline and Revival === After periods of decline, the temple was restored during the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享年間|12=draft}} by the monk Ben’i. The belief that "pilgrimage to Aonomine protects against storms" spread, leading to a revival supported by the maritime trade of the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}. Shōfuku-ji reached its peak during the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1831), supported by merchants and seafarers from regions like Owari, Osaka, Ise, and Toba. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1125 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister {{ill|Justin Trudeau|ja|ジャスティン・トルドー|12=draft}} visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the {{ill|42nd G7 Summit|ja|第42回先進国首脳会議|12=draft}} in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either {{ill|Matsuo Station|ja|松尾駅|12=draft}} or {{ill|Kutsukake Station|ja|沓掛駅|12=draft}}, both served by {{ill|Kintetsu Shima Line|ja|近鉄志摩線|12=draft}}. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Shoufukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Shingon Buddhism]] [[Category:Cultural properties in Japan]] [[Category:Toba City]] [[Category:Mie Shikoku Pilgrimage]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Q11]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[sn:Shoufukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[ja:正福寺 (鳥羽市)]] [[sn:houfukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[en:Shoufukuji Temple (Toba City)]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shoufukuji Temple (Toba City)}}
