revision.text:
{{infobox religious building | name = Shōfuku-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Shouhukuji (Toba,Mie) 02.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall | location = [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] Matsuo-cho 519 | LatitudeDegrees = 34 | LatitudeMinutes = 24 | LatitudeSeconds = 37.4 | LongitudeDegrees = 136 | LongitudeMinutes = 49 | LongitudeSeconds = 35.5 | Map = Japan#Japan Mie | Mountainname = Aonomine-san | Sect = [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhism]] | Subsect = Kōyasan Shingon | TempleRank = | Principaldeity = [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Faced Kannon]] | Yearconsecration_year = During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era | Founder = [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]] (traditionally attributed) | Restorer = During the Jōkyō era | Alias = Aonomine-san, Saga Palace | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|伊勢西国三十三所観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=}} (Non-numbered Temple)
{{ill|Tōkai-Kinki Jizō Pilgrimage|ja|東海近畿地蔵霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai-Kinki Jizō Pilgrimage|qq=}} (No. 17)
{{ill|Tōkai 36 Fudō Pilgrimage|ja|東海三十六不動尊霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai 36 Fudō Pilgrimage|qq=}} (No. 23)
{{ill|Mie Shikoku 88 Temples|ja|三重四国八十八箇所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Shikoku 88 Temples|qq=}} (No. 88) | CulturalProperty = Two Toba City-designated cultural properties | OfficialWebsite = }} '''Shōfuku-ji''' (正福寺) is a Buddhist temple of the [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon]] sect, located in [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. Its mountain name is Aonomine-san (青峯山), situated near the summit of a 336-meter-high mountain of the same name.{{Cite web|url=https://isekannon.jp/wp-content/themes/isesaigoku2020/files/images/common/panf.pdf|title=Ise Saigoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|publisher=Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage Office|access-date=December 17, 2024}} It is also known by the alias "Saga Palace." Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 703 The temple is a sacred site for maritime safety, attracting fervent devotion from those connected to the fishing industry.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 818 Worshippers range from [[:en:Obira, Hokkaido|Obira Town]], Hokkaido, to [[:en:Kagoshima|Kagoshima City]], Kagoshima.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 706 == Overview == Shōfuku-ji is located in a valley slightly below the peak of Aonomine-san (known as Tenato-yama) at the border of [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]] and [[:en:Shima, Mie|Shima City]]. Commonly referred to as "Aonomine-san," it is a rare [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhist temple]] in the {{ill|Shima Peninsula|ja|志摩地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shima Peninsula|qq=}}, an area dominated by [[:en:Sōtō|Sōtō Zen]] temples.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 999 === Grounds === Within the mountain gate (constructed over 30 years, according to legend), there are several buildings, including the Main Hall (Kondō), Shōten Hall, Daishi Hall, Benzaiten Hall, Nyoirin Hall, Guest Hall, and the kitchen area.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1041 Visitors are allowed to ring the temple bell freely.Kinki Nippon Tourist Publishing Center (2005): p. 103 The Main Hall was built in 1836.[http://www.takenaka.co.jp/majorworks/30050111836-01.html?keepThis=true&TB_iframe=true (Takenaka Corporation)] === Principal deity === The temple enshrines the [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Faced Kannon]] as its principal deity. The statue is revealed during the {{ill|Ofune Matsuri|ja|御船祭|Ofune Matsuri=Ofune Matsuri|lt=Ofune Festival|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ofune Matsuri|qq=}}. According to legend, the Kannon statue was a golden Buddha that appeared on the back of a whale from the sea off {{ill|Ousatsu|ja|相差村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ousatsu|qq=}} (present-day [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]], Osatsu). The site where the whale surfaced is known as "Whale Cape," and the whale itself is said to have turned to stone, now referred to as "Whale Stone."{{Cite web|url=http://www.mietv.com/yajikita09/archive/090408/04.html|title=Whale Cape Promenade|publisher=Mie TV|access-date=May 15, 2011}} The statue was later visited by a high-Ranking monk known as Baramon Sōjō, who carved a wooden image to enshrine the luminous statue within.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1040 == History == === Legendary Origins === According to the "Shōfuku-ji Engi," the temple was founded during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] by [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]]. During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era (749–757), Gyōki received a divine oracle at [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], instructing him to build a temple at "Tenrō Mine" (青峯), promising boundless spiritual benefits.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1039 In 806 CE, [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] returned from Tang China and, in 807 CE, under imperial edict, converted the temple to the [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon sect]]. === Decline and Revival === After periods of decline, the temple was restored during the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享年間|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōkyō era|qq=}} by the monk Ben’i. The belief that "pilgrimage to Aonomine protects against storms" spread, leading to a revival supported by the maritime trade of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Shōfuku-ji reached its peak during the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1831), supported by merchants and seafarers from regions like Owari, Osaka, Ise, and Toba. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1125 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister [[:en:Justin Trudeau|Justin Trudeau]] visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the [[:en:42nd G7 summit|42nd G7 Summit]] in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either [[:en:Matsuo Station|Matsuo Station]] or [[:en:Kutsukake Station|Kutsukake Station]], both served by [[:en:Shima Line|Kintetsu Shima Line]]. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. {{translated page|ja|正福寺 (鳥羽市)|version=103349905|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Shingon Buddhism]] [[Category:Cultural properties in Japan]] }} [[sn:Shoufukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[ja:正福寺 (鳥羽市)]] [[sn:houfukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[en:Shoufukuji Temple (Toba City)]]

The site where the whale surfaced is known as "Whale Cape," and the whale itself is said to have turned to stone, now referred to as "Whale Stone."{{Cite web|url=http://www.mietv.com/yajikita09/archive/090408/04.html|title=Whale Cape Promenade|publisher=Mie TV|access-date=May 15, 2011}} The statue was later visited by a high-Ranking monk known as Baramon Sōjō, who carved a wooden image to enshrine the luminous statue within.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1040 == History == === Legendary Origins === According to the "Shōfuku-ji Engi," the temple was founded during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] by [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]]. During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era (749–757), Gyōki received a divine oracle at [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], instructing him to build a temple at "Tenrō Mine" (青峯), promising boundless spiritual benefits.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1039 In 806 CE, [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] returned from Tang China and, in 807 CE, under imperial edict, converted the temple to the [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon sect]]. === Decline and Revival === After periods of decline, the temple was restored during the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享年間|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōkyō era|qq=}} by the monk Ben’i. The belief that "pilgrimage to Aonomine protects against storms" spread, leading to a revival supported by the maritime trade of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Shōfuku-ji reached its peak during the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1831), supported by merchants and seafarers from regions like Owari, Osaka, Ise, and Toba. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1125 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister [[:en:Justin Trudeau|Justin Trudeau]] visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the [[:en:42nd G7 summit|42nd G7 Summit]] in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either [[:en:Matsuo Station|Matsuo Station]] or [[:en:Kutsukake Station|Kutsukake Station]], both served by [[:en:Shima Line|Kintetsu Shima Line]]. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. {{translated page|ja|正福寺 (鳥羽市)|version=103349905|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Shingon Buddhism]] [[Category:Cultural properties in Japan]] }} [[sn:Shoufukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[ja:正福寺 (鳥羽市)]] [[sn:houfukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[en:Shoufukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[Category:三重四国八十八箇所]] [[Category:三重県の漁業]]

revision.text:
{{infobox religious building | name = Shōfuku-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Shouhukuji (Toba,Mie) 02.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall | location = [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] Matsuo-cho 519 | LatitudeDegrees = 34 | LatitudeMinutes = 24 | LatitudeSeconds = 37.4 | LongitudeDegrees = 136 | LongitudeMinutes = 49 | LongitudeSeconds = 35.5 | Map = Japan#Japan Mie | Mountainname = Aonomine-san | Sect = [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhism]] | Subsect = Kōyasan Shingon | TempleRank = | Principaldeity = [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Faced Kannon]] | Yearconsecration_year = During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era | Founder = [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]] (traditionally attributed) | Restorer = During the Jōkyō era | Alias = Aonomine-san, Saga Palace | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|伊勢西国三十三所観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=}} (Non-numbered Temple)
{{ill|Tōkai-Kinki Jizō Pilgrimage|ja|東海近畿地蔵霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai-Kinki Jizō Pilgrimage|qq=}} (No. 17)
{{ill|Tōkai 36 Fudō Pilgrimage|ja|東海三十六不動尊霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai 36 Fudō Pilgrimage|qq=}} (No. 23)
{{ill|Mie Shikoku 88 Temples|ja|三重四国八十八箇所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Shikoku 88 Temples|qq=}} (No. 88) | CulturalProperty = Two Toba City-designated cultural properties | OfficialWebsite = }} '''Shōfuku-ji''' (正福寺) is a Buddhist temple of the [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon]] sect, located in [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. Its mountain name is Aonomine-san (青峯山), situated near the summit of a 336-meter-high mountain of the same name.{{Cite web|url=https://isekannon.jp/wp-content/themes/isesaigoku2020/files/images/common/panf.pdf|title=Ise Saigoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|publisher=Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage Office|access-date=December 17, 2024}} It is also known by the alias "Saga Palace." Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 703 The temple is a sacred site for maritime safety, attracting fervent devotion from those connected to the fishing industry.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 818 Worshippers range from [[:en:Obira, Hokkaido|Obira Town]], Hokkaido, to [[:en:Kagoshima|Kagoshima City]], Kagoshima.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 706 == Overview == Shōfuku-ji is located in a valley slightly below the peak of Aonomine-san (known as Tenato-yama) at the border of [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]] and [[:en:Shima, Mie|Shima City]]. Commonly referred to as "Aonomine-san," it is a rare [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhist temple]] in the {{ill|Shima Peninsula|ja|志摩地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shima Peninsula|qq=}}, an area dominated by [[:en:Sōtō|Sōtō Zen]] temples.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 999 === Grounds === Within the mountain gate (constructed over 30 years, according to legend), there are several buildings, including the Main Hall (Kondō), Shōten Hall, Daishi Hall, Benzaiten Hall, Nyoirin Hall, Guest Hall, and the kitchen area.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1041 Visitors are allowed to ring the temple bell freely.Kinki Nippon Tourist Publishing Center (2005): p. 103 The Main Hall was built in 1836.[http://www.takenaka.co.jp/majorworks/30050111836-01.html?keepThis=true&TB_iframe=true (Takenaka Corporation)] === Principal deity === The temple enshrines the [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Faced Kannon]] as its principal deity. The statue is revealed during the {{ill|Ofune Matsuri|ja|御船祭|Ofune Matsuri=Ofune Matsuri|lt=Ofune Festival|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ofune Matsuri|qq=}}. According to legend, the Kannon statue was a golden Buddha that appeared on the back of a whale from the sea off {{ill|Ousatsu|ja|相差村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ousatsu|qq=}} (present-day [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]], Osatsu). The site where the whale surfaced is known as "Whale Cape," and the whale itself is said to have turned to stone, now referred to as "Whale Stone."{{Cite web|url=http://www.mietv.com/yajikita09/archive/090408/04.html|title=Whale Cape Promenade|publisher=Mie TV|access-date=May 15, 2011}} The statue was later visited by a high-Ranking monk known as Baramon Sōjō, who carved a wooden image to enshrine the luminous statue within.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1040 == History == === Legendary Origins === According to the "Shōfuku-ji Engi," the temple was founded during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] by [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]]. During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era (749–757), Gyōki received a divine oracle at [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], instructing him to build a temple at "Tenrō Mine" (青峯), promising boundless spiritual benefits.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1039 In 806 CE, [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] returned from Tang China and, in 807 CE, under imperial edict, converted the temple to the [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon sect]]. === Decline and Revival === After periods of decline, the temple was restored during the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享年間|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōkyō era|qq=}} by the monk Ben’i. The belief that "pilgrimage to Aonomine protects against storms" spread, leading to a revival supported by the maritime trade of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Shōfuku-ji reached its peak during the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1831), supported by merchants and seafarers from regions like Owari, Osaka, Ise, and Toba. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1125 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister [[:en:Justin Trudeau|Justin Trudeau]] visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the [[:en:42nd G7 summit|42nd G7 Summit]] in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either [[:en:Matsuo Station|Matsuo Station]] or [[:en:Kutsukake Station|Kutsukake Station]], both served by [[:en:Shima Line|Kintetsu Shima Line]]. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. {{translated page|ja|正福寺 (鳥羽市)|version=103349905|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Shingon Buddhism]] [[Category:Cultural properties in Japan]] }} [[sn:Shoufukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[ja:正福寺 (鳥羽市)]] [[sn:houfukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[en:Shoufukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[Category:三重四国八十八箇所]] [[Category:三重県の漁業]] [[Category:三重県内の市町村指定有形文化財]]

The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1125 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister [[:en:Justin Trudeau|Justin Trudeau]] visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the [[:en:42nd G7 summit|42nd G7 Summit]] in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either [[:en:Matsuo Station|Matsuo Station]] or [[:en:Kutsukake Station|Kutsukake Station]], both served by [[:en:Shima Line|Kintetsu Shima Line]]. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. {{translated page|ja|正福寺 (鳥羽市)|version=103349905|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Shingon Buddhism]] [[Category:Cultural properties in Japan]] }} [[sn:Shoufukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[ja:正福寺 (鳥羽市)]] [[sn:houfukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[en:Shoufukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[Category:三重四国八十八箇所]] [[Category:三重県の漁業]] [[Category:三重県内の市町村指定有形文化財]] [[Category:Water transport in Japan]] [[Category:東海三十六不動尊霊場]]

revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:Edo period architecture
revision.text:
{{infobox religious building | name = Shōfuku-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Shouhukuji (Toba,Mie) 02.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall | location = [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] Matsuo-cho 519 | LatitudeDegrees = 34 | LatitudeMinutes = 24 | LatitudeSeconds = 37.4 | LongitudeDegrees = 136 | LongitudeMinutes = 49 | LongitudeSeconds = 35.5 | Map = Japan#Japan Mie | Mountainname = Aonomine-san | Sect = [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhism]] | Subsect = Kōyasan Shingon | TempleRank = | Principaldeity = [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Faced Kannon]] | Yearconsecration_year = During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era | Founder = [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]] (traditionally attributed) | Restorer = During the Jōkyō era | Alias = Aonomine-san, Saga Palace | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|伊勢西国三十三所観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=}} (Non-numbered Temple)
{{ill|Tōkai-Kinki Jizō Pilgrimage|ja|東海近畿地蔵霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai-Kinki Jizō Pilgrimage|qq=}} (No. 17)
{{ill|Tōkai 36 Fudō Pilgrimage|ja|東海三十六不動尊霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai 36 Fudō Pilgrimage|qq=}} (No. 23)
{{ill|Mie Shikoku 88 Temples|ja|三重四国八十八箇所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Shikoku 88 Temples|qq=}} (No. 88) | CulturalProperty = Two Toba City-designated cultural properties | OfficialWebsite = }} '''Shōfuku-ji''' (正福寺) is a Buddhist temple of the [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon]] sect, located in [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. Its mountain name is Aonomine-san (青峯山), situated near the summit of a 336-meter-high mountain of the same name.{{Cite web|url=https://isekannon.jp/wp-content/themes/isesaigoku2020/files/images/common/panf.pdf|title=Ise Saigoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|publisher=Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage Office|access-date=December 17, 2024}} It is also known by the alias "Saga Palace." Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 703 The temple is a sacred site for maritime safety, attracting fervent devotion from those connected to the fishing industry.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 818 Worshippers range from [[:en:Obira, Hokkaido|Obira Town]], Hokkaido, to [[:en:Kagoshima|Kagoshima City]], Kagoshima.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 706 == Overview == Shōfuku-ji is located in a valley slightly below the peak of Aonomine-san (known as Tenato-yama) at the border of [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]] and [[:en:Shima, Mie|Shima City]]. Commonly referred to as "Aonomine-san," it is a rare [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhist temple]] in the {{ill|Shima Peninsula|ja|志摩地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shima Peninsula|qq=}}, an area dominated by [[:en:Sōtō|Sōtō Zen]] temples.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 999 === Grounds === Within the mountain gate (constructed over 30 years, according to legend), there are several buildings, including the Main Hall (Kondō), Shōten Hall, Daishi Hall, Benzaiten Hall, Nyoirin Hall, Guest Hall, and the kitchen area.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1041 Visitors are allowed to ring the temple bell freely.Kinki Nippon Tourist Publishing Center (2005): p. 103 The Main Hall was built in 1836.[http://www.takenaka.co.jp/majorworks/30050111836-01.html?keepThis=true&TB_iframe=true (Takenaka Corporation)] === Principal deity === The temple enshrines the [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Faced Kannon]] as its principal deity. The statue is revealed during the {{ill|Ofune Matsuri|ja|御船祭|Ofune Matsuri=Ofune Matsuri|lt=Ofune Festival|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ofune Matsuri|qq=}}. According to legend, the Kannon statue was a golden Buddha that appeared on the back of a whale from the sea off {{ill|Ousatsu|ja|相差村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ousatsu|qq=}} (present-day [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]], Osatsu). The site where the whale surfaced is known as "Whale Cape," and the whale itself is said to have turned to stone, now referred to as "Whale Stone."{{Cite web|url=http://www.mietv.com/yajikita09/archive/090408/04.html|title=Whale Cape Promenade|publisher=Mie TV|access-date=May 15, 2011}} The statue was later visited by a high-Ranking monk known as Baramon Sōjō, who carved a wooden image to enshrine the luminous statue within.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1040 == History == === Legendary Origins === According to the "Shōfuku-ji Engi," the temple was founded during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] by [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]]. During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era (749–757), Gyōki received a divine oracle at [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], instructing him to build a temple at "Tenrō Mine" (青峯), promising boundless spiritual benefits.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1039 In 806 CE, [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] returned from Tang China and, in 807 CE, under imperial edict, converted the temple to the [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon sect]]. === Decline and Revival === After periods of decline, the temple was restored during the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享年間|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōkyō era|qq=}} by the monk Ben’i. The belief that "pilgrimage to Aonomine protects against storms" spread, leading to a revival supported by the maritime trade of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Shōfuku-ji reached its peak during the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1831), supported by merchants and seafarers from regions like Owari, Osaka, Ise, and Toba. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1125 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister [[:en:Justin Trudeau|Justin Trudeau]] visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the [[:en:42nd G7 summit|42nd G7 Summit]] in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either [[:en:Matsuo Station|Matsuo Station]] or [[:en:Kutsukake Station|Kutsukake Station]], both served by [[:en:Shima Line|Kintetsu Shima Line]]. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. {{translated page|ja|正福寺 (鳥羽市)|version=103349905|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Shingon Buddhism]] [[Category:Cultural properties in Japan]] }} [[sn:Shoufukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[ja:正福寺 (鳥羽市)]] [[sn:houfukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[en:Shoufukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[Category:三重四国八十八箇所]] [[Category:三重県の漁業]] [[Category:三重県内の市町村指定有形文化財]] [[Category:Water transport in Japan]] [[Category:東海三十六不動尊霊場]] [[Category:Edo period architecture]]

For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. {{translated page|ja|正福寺 (鳥羽市)|version=103349905|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Shingon Buddhism]] [[Category:Cultural properties in Japan]] }} [[sn:Shoufukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[ja:正福寺 (鳥羽市)]] [[sn:houfukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[en:Shoufukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[Category:三重四国八十八箇所]] [[Category:三重県の漁業]] [[Category:三重県内の市町村指定有形文化財]] [[Category:Water transport in Japan]] [[Category:東海三十六不動尊霊場]] [[Category:Edo period architecture]] [[Category:Kōyasan Shingon temples]] [[Category:鳥羽市の建築物]]

revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:鳥羽市の歴史
revision.text:
{{infobox religious building | name = Shōfuku-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Shouhukuji (Toba,Mie) 02.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall | location = [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] Matsuo-cho 519 | LatitudeDegrees = 34 | LatitudeMinutes = 24 | LatitudeSeconds = 37.4 | LongitudeDegrees = 136 | LongitudeMinutes = 49 | LongitudeSeconds = 35.5 | Map = Japan#Japan Mie | Mountainname = Aonomine-san | Sect = [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhism]] | Subsect = Kōyasan Shingon | TempleRank = | Principaldeity = [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Faced Kannon]] | Yearconsecration_year = During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era | Founder = [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]] (traditionally attributed) | Restorer = During the Jōkyō era | Alias = Aonomine-san, Saga Palace | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|伊勢西国三十三所観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=}} (Non-numbered Temple)
{{ill|Tōkai-Kinki Jizō Pilgrimage|ja|東海近畿地蔵霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai-Kinki Jizō Pilgrimage|qq=}} (No. 17)
{{ill|Tōkai 36 Fudō Pilgrimage|ja|東海三十六不動尊霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai 36 Fudō Pilgrimage|qq=}} (No. 23)
{{ill|Mie Shikoku 88 Temples|ja|三重四国八十八箇所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Shikoku 88 Temples|qq=}} (No. 88) | CulturalProperty = Two Toba City-designated cultural properties | OfficialWebsite = }} '''Shōfuku-ji''' (正福寺) is a Buddhist temple of the [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon]] sect, located in [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. Its mountain name is Aonomine-san (青峯山), situated near the summit of a 336-meter-high mountain of the same name.{{Cite web|url=https://isekannon.jp/wp-content/themes/isesaigoku2020/files/images/common/panf.pdf|title=Ise Saigoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|publisher=Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage Office|access-date=December 17, 2024}} It is also known by the alias "Saga Palace." Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 703 The temple is a sacred site for maritime safety, attracting fervent devotion from those connected to the fishing industry.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 818 Worshippers range from [[:en:Obira, Hokkaido|Obira Town]], Hokkaido, to [[:en:Kagoshima|Kagoshima City]], Kagoshima.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 706 == Overview == Shōfuku-ji is located in a valley slightly below the peak of Aonomine-san (known as Tenato-yama) at the border of [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]] and [[:en:Shima, Mie|Shima City]]. Commonly referred to as "Aonomine-san," it is a rare [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhist temple]] in the {{ill|Shima Peninsula|ja|志摩地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shima Peninsula|qq=}}, an area dominated by [[:en:Sōtō|Sōtō Zen]] temples.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 999 === Grounds === Within the mountain gate (constructed over 30 years, according to legend), there are several buildings, including the Main Hall (Kondō), Shōten Hall, Daishi Hall, Benzaiten Hall, Nyoirin Hall, Guest Hall, and the kitchen area.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1041 Visitors are allowed to ring the temple bell freely.Kinki Nippon Tourist Publishing Center (2005): p. 103 The Main Hall was built in 1836.[http://www.takenaka.co.jp/majorworks/30050111836-01.html?keepThis=true&TB_iframe=true (Takenaka Corporation)] === Principal deity === The temple enshrines the [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Faced Kannon]] as its principal deity. The statue is revealed during the {{ill|Ofune Matsuri|ja|御船祭|Ofune Matsuri=Ofune Matsuri|lt=Ofune Festival|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ofune Matsuri|qq=}}. According to legend, the Kannon statue was a golden Buddha that appeared on the back of a whale from the sea off {{ill|Ousatsu|ja|相差村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ousatsu|qq=}} (present-day [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]], Osatsu). The site where the whale surfaced is known as "Whale Cape," and the whale itself is said to have turned to stone, now referred to as "Whale Stone."{{Cite web|url=http://www.mietv.com/yajikita09/archive/090408/04.html|title=Whale Cape Promenade|publisher=Mie TV|access-date=May 15, 2011}} The statue was later visited by a high-Ranking monk known as Baramon Sōjō, who carved a wooden image to enshrine the luminous statue within.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1040 == History == === Legendary Origins === According to the "Shōfuku-ji Engi," the temple was founded during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] by [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]]. During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era (749–757), Gyōki received a divine oracle at [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], instructing him to build a temple at "Tenrō Mine" (青峯), promising boundless spiritual benefits.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1039 In 806 CE, [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] returned from Tang China and, in 807 CE, under imperial edict, converted the temple to the [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon sect]]. === Decline and Revival === After periods of decline, the temple was restored during the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享年間|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōkyō era|qq=}} by the monk Ben’i. The belief that "pilgrimage to Aonomine protects against storms" spread, leading to a revival supported by the maritime trade of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Shōfuku-ji reached its peak during the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1831), supported by merchants and seafarers from regions like Owari, Osaka, Ise, and Toba. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1125 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister [[:en:Justin Trudeau|Justin Trudeau]] visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the [[:en:42nd G7 summit|42nd G7 Summit]] in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either [[:en:Matsuo Station|Matsuo Station]] or [[:en:Kutsukake Station|Kutsukake Station]], both served by [[:en:Shima Line|Kintetsu Shima Line]]. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. {{translated page|ja|正福寺 (鳥羽市)|version=103349905|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Shingon Buddhism]] [[Category:Cultural properties in Japan]] }} [[sn:Shoufukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[ja:正福寺 (鳥羽市)]] [[sn:houfukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[en:Shoufukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[Category:三重四国八十八箇所]] [[Category:三重県の漁業]] [[Category:三重県内の市町村指定有形文化財]] [[Category:Water transport in Japan]] [[Category:東海三十六不動尊霊場]] [[Category:Edo period architecture]] [[Category:Kōyasan Shingon temples]] [[Category:鳥羽市の建築物]] [[Category:鳥羽市の歴史]]

revision.text:
{{infobox religious building | name = Shōfuku-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Shouhukuji (Toba,Mie) 02.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall | location = [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] Matsuo-cho 519 | LatitudeDegrees = 34 | LatitudeMinutes = 24 | LatitudeSeconds = 37.4 | LongitudeDegrees = 136 | LongitudeMinutes = 49 | LongitudeSeconds = 35.5 | Map = Japan#Japan Mie | Mountainname = Aonomine-san | Sect = [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhism]] | Subsect = Kōyasan Shingon | TempleRank = | Principaldeity = [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Faced Kannon]] | Yearconsecration_year = During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era | Founder = [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]] (traditionally attributed) | Restorer = During the Jōkyō era | Alias = Aonomine-san, Saga Palace | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|伊勢西国三十三所観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=}} (Non-numbered Temple)
{{ill|Tōkai-Kinki Jizō Pilgrimage|ja|東海近畿地蔵霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai-Kinki Jizō Pilgrimage|qq=}} (No. 17)
{{ill|Tōkai 36 Fudō Pilgrimage|ja|東海三十六不動尊霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai 36 Fudō Pilgrimage|qq=}} (No. 23)
{{ill|Mie Shikoku 88 Temples|ja|三重四国八十八箇所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Shikoku 88 Temples|qq=}} (No. 88) | CulturalProperty = Two Toba City-designated cultural properties | OfficialWebsite = }} '''Shōfuku-ji''' (正福寺) is a Buddhist temple of the [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon]] sect, located in [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. Its mountain name is Aonomine-san (青峯山), situated near the summit of a 336-meter-high mountain of the same name.{{Cite web|url=https://isekannon.jp/wp-content/themes/isesaigoku2020/files/images/common/panf.pdf|title=Ise Saigoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|publisher=Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage Office|access-date=December 17, 2024}} It is also known by the alias "Saga Palace." Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 703 The temple is a sacred site for maritime safety, attracting fervent devotion from those connected to the fishing industry.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 818 Worshippers range from [[:en:Obira, Hokkaido|Obira Town]], Hokkaido, to [[:en:Kagoshima|Kagoshima City]], Kagoshima.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 706 == Overview == Shōfuku-ji is located in a valley slightly below the peak of Aonomine-san (known as Tenato-yama) at the border of [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]] and [[:en:Shima, Mie|Shima City]]. Commonly referred to as "Aonomine-san," it is a rare [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhist temple]] in the {{ill|Shima Peninsula|ja|志摩地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shima Peninsula|qq=}}, an area dominated by [[:en:Sōtō|Sōtō Zen]] temples.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 999 === Grounds === Within the mountain gate (constructed over 30 years, according to legend), there are several buildings, including the Main Hall (Kondō), Shōten Hall, Daishi Hall, Benzaiten Hall, Nyoirin Hall, Guest Hall, and the kitchen area.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1041 Visitors are allowed to ring the temple bell freely.Kinki Nippon Tourist Publishing Center (2005): p. 103 The Main Hall was built in 1836.[http://www.takenaka.co.jp/majorworks/30050111836-01.html?keepThis=true&TB_iframe=true (Takenaka Corporation)] === Principal deity === The temple enshrines the [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Faced Kannon]] as its principal deity. The statue is revealed during the {{ill|Ofune Matsuri|ja|御船祭|Ofune Matsuri=Ofune Matsuri|lt=Ofune Festival|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ofune Matsuri|qq=}}. According to legend, the Kannon statue was a golden Buddha that appeared on the back of a whale from the sea off {{ill|Ousatsu|ja|相差村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ousatsu|qq=}} (present-day [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]], Osatsu). The site where the whale surfaced is known as "Whale Cape," and the whale itself is said to have turned to stone, now referred to as "Whale Stone."{{Cite web|url=http://www.mietv.com/yajikita09/archive/090408/04.html|title=Whale Cape Promenade|publisher=Mie TV|access-date=May 15, 2011}} The statue was later visited by a high-Ranking monk known as Baramon Sōjō, who carved a wooden image to enshrine the luminous statue within.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1040 == History == === Legendary Origins === According to the "Shōfuku-ji Engi," the temple was founded during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] by [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]]. During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era (749–757), Gyōki received a divine oracle at [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], instructing him to build a temple at "Tenrō Mine" (青峯), promising boundless spiritual benefits.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1039 In 806 CE, [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] returned from Tang China and, in 807 CE, under imperial edict, converted the temple to the [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon sect]]. === Decline and Revival === After periods of decline, the temple was restored during the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享年間|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōkyō era|qq=}} by the monk Ben’i. The belief that "pilgrimage to Aonomine protects against storms" spread, leading to a revival supported by the maritime trade of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Shōfuku-ji reached its peak during the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1831), supported by merchants and seafarers from regions like Owari, Osaka, Ise, and Toba. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1125 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister [[:en:Justin Trudeau|Justin Trudeau]] visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the [[:en:42nd G7 summit|42nd G7 Summit]] in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either [[:en:Matsuo Station|Matsuo Station]] or [[:en:Kutsukake Station|Kutsukake Station]], both served by [[:en:Shima Line|Kintetsu Shima Line]]. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. {{translated page|ja|正福寺 (鳥羽市)|version=103349905|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Shingon Buddhism]] [[Category:Cultural properties in Japan]] }} [[sn:Shoufukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[ja:正福寺 (鳥羽市)]] [[sn:houfukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[en:Shoufukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[Category:三重四国八十八箇所]] [[Category:Fisheries of Mie prefecture]] [[Category:三重県内の市町村指定有形文化財]] [[Category:Water transport in Japan]] [[Category:東海三十六不動尊霊場]] [[Category:Edo period architecture]] [[Category:Kōyasan Shingon temples]] [[Category:Buildings in Toba, Mie]] [[Category:History of Toba, Mie]]

Main Hall | location = [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] Matsuo-cho 519 | LatitudeDegrees = 34 | LatitudeMinutes = 24 | LatitudeSeconds = 37.4 | LongitudeDegrees = 136 | LongitudeMinutes = 49 | LongitudeSeconds = 35.5 | Map = Japan#Japan Mie | Mountainname = Aonomine-san | Sect = [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhism]] | Subsect = Kōyasan Shingon | TempleRank = | Principaldeity = [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Faced Kannon]] | Yearconsecration_year = During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era | Founder = [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]] (traditionally attributed) | Restorer = During the Jōkyō era | Alias = Aonomine-san, Saga Palace | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|伊勢西国三十三所観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=}} (Non-numbered Temple)
{{ill|Tōkai-Kinki Jizō Pilgrimage|ja|東海近畿地蔵霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai-Kinki Jizō Pilgrimage|qq=}} (No. 17)
{{ill|Tōkai 36 Fudō Pilgrimage|ja|東海三十六不動尊霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai 36 Fudō Pilgrimage|qq=}} (No. 23)
{{ill|Mie Shikoku 88 Temples|ja|三重四国八十八箇所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Shikoku 88 Temples|qq=}} (No. 88) | CulturalProperty = Two Toba City-designated cultural properties | OfficialWebsite = }} '''Shōfuku-ji''' (正福寺) is a Buddhist temple of the [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon]] sect, located in [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. Its mountain name is Aonomine-san (青峯山), situated near the summit of a 336-meter-high mountain of the same name.{{Cite web|url=https://isekannon.jp/wp-content/themes/isesaigoku2020/files/images/common/panf.pdf|title=Ise Saigoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|publisher=Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage Office|access-date=December 17, 2024}} It is also known by the alias "Saga Palace." Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 703 The temple is a sacred site for maritime safety, attracting fervent devotion from those connected to the fishing industry.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 818 Worshippers range from [[:en:Obira, Hokkaido|Obira Town]], Hokkaido, to [[:en:Kagoshima|Kagoshima City]], Kagoshima.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 706 == Overview == Shōfuku-ji is located in a valley slightly below the peak of Aonomine-san (known as Tenato-yama) at the border of [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]] and [[:en:Shima, Mie|Shima City]]. Commonly referred to as "Aonomine-san," it is a rare [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhist temple]] in the {{ill|Shima Peninsula|ja|志摩地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shima Peninsula|qq=}}, an area dominated by [[:en:Sōtō|Sōtō Zen]] temples.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 999 === Grounds === Within the mountain gate (constructed over 30 years, according to legend), there are several buildings, including the Main Hall (Kondō), Shōten Hall, Daishi Hall, Benzaiten Hall, Nyoirin Hall, Guest Hall, and the kitchen area.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1041 Visitors are allowed to ring the temple bell freely.Kinki Nippon Tourist Publishing Center (2005): p. 103 The Main Hall was built in 1836.[http://www.takenaka.co.jp/majorworks/30050111836-01.html?keepThis=true&TB_iframe=true (Takenaka Corporation)] === Principal deity === The temple enshrines the [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Faced Kannon]] as its principal deity. The statue is revealed during the {{ill|Ofune Matsuri|ja|御船祭|Ofune Matsuri=Ofune Matsuri|lt=Ofune Festival|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ofune Matsuri|qq=}}. According to legend, the Kannon statue was a golden Buddha that appeared on the back of a whale from the sea off {{ill|Ousatsu|ja|相差村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ousatsu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (present-day [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]], Osatsu). The site where the whale surfaced is known as "Whale Cape," and the whale itself is said to have turned to stone, now referred to as "Whale Stone."{{Cite web|url=http://www.mietv.com/yajikita09/archive/090408/04.html|title=Whale Cape Promenade|publisher=Mie TV|access-date=May 15, 2011}} The statue was later visited by a high-Ranking monk known as Baramon Sōjō, who carved a wooden image to enshrine the luminous statue within.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1040 == History == === Legendary Origins === According to the "Shōfuku-ji Engi," the temple was founded during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] by [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]]. During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era (749–757), Gyōki received a divine oracle at [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], instructing him to build a temple at "Tenrō Mine" (青峯), promising boundless spiritual benefits.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1039 In 806 CE, [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] returned from Tang China and, in 807 CE, under imperial edict, converted the temple to the [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon sect]]. === Decline and Revival === After periods of decline, the temple was restored during the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享年間|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōkyō era|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} by the monk Ben’i. The belief that "pilgrimage to Aonomine protects against storms" spread, leading to a revival supported by the maritime trade of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Shōfuku-ji reached its peak during the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1831), supported by merchants and seafarers from regions like Owari, Osaka, Ise, and Toba. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1125 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister [[:en:Justin Trudeau|Justin Trudeau]] visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the [[:en:42nd G7 summit|42nd G7 Summit]] in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either [[:en:Matsuo Station|Matsuo Station]] or [[:en:Kutsukake Station|Kutsukake Station]], both served by [[:en:Shima Line|Kintetsu Shima Line]]. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. {{translated page|ja|正福寺 (鳥羽市)|version=103349905|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Shingon Buddhism]] [[Category:Cultural properties in Japan]] }} [[sn:Shoufukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[ja:正福寺 (鳥羽市)]] [[sn:houfukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[Category:三重四国八十八箇所]] [[Category:Fisheries of Mie prefecture]] [[Category:三重県内の市町村指定有形文化財]] [[Category:Water transport in Japan]] [[Category:東海三十六不動尊霊場]] [[Category:Edo period architecture]] [[Category:Kōyasan Shingon temples]] [[Category:Buildings in Toba, Mie]] [[Category:History of Toba, Mie]] {{Commons category|Shōfuku-ji (Toba)}}

{{infobox religious building | name = Shōfuku-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Shouhukuji (Toba,Mie) 02.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall | location = [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] Matsuo-cho 519 | LatitudeDegrees = 34 | LatitudeMinutes = 24 | LatitudeSeconds = 37.4 | LongitudeDegrees = 136 | LongitudeMinutes = 49 | LongitudeSeconds = 35.5 | Map = Japan#Japan Mie | Mountainname = Aonomine-san | Sect = [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhism]] | Subsect = Kōyasan Shingon | TempleRank = | Principaldeity = [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Faced Kannon]] | Yearconsecration_year = During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era | Founder = [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]] (traditionally attributed) | Restorer = During the Jōkyō era | Alias = Aonomine-san, Saga Palace | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|伊勢西国三十三所観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=}} (Non-numbered Temple)
{{ill|Tōkai-Kinki Jizō Pilgrimage|ja|東海近畿地蔵霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai-Kinki Jizō Pilgrimage|qq=}} (No. 17)
{{ill|Tōkai 36 Fudō Pilgrimage|ja|東海三十六不動尊霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai 36 Fudō Pilgrimage|qq=}} (No. 23)
{{ill|Mie Shikoku 88 Temples|ja|三重四国八十八箇所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Shikoku 88 Temples|qq=}} (No. 88) | CulturalProperty = Two Toba City-designated cultural properties | OfficialWebsite = }} '''Shōfuku-ji''' (正福寺) is a Buddhist temple of the [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon]] sect, located in [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. Its mountain name is Aonomine-san (青峯山), situated near the summit of a 336-meter-high mountain of the same name.{{Cite web|url=https://isekannon.jp/wp-content/themes/isesaigoku2020/files/images/common/panf.pdf|title=Ise Saigoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|publisher=Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage Office|access-date=December 17, 2024}} It is also known by the alias "Saga Palace." Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 703 The temple is a sacred site for maritime safety, attracting fervent devotion from those connected to the fishing industry.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 818 Worshippers range from [[:en:Obira, Hokkaido|Obira Town]], Hokkaido, to [[:en:Kagoshima|Kagoshima City]], Kagoshima.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 706 == Overview == Shōfuku-ji is located in a valley slightly below the peak of Aonomine-san (known as Tenato-yama) at the border of [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]] and [[:en:Shima, Mie|Shima City]]. Commonly referred to as "Aonomine-san," it is a rare [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhist temple]] in the {{ill|Shima Peninsula|ja|志摩地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shima Peninsula|qq=}}, an area dominated by [[:en:Sōtō|Sōtō Zen]] temples.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 999 === Grounds === Within the mountain gate (constructed over 30 years, according to legend), there are several buildings, including the Main Hall (Kondō), Shōten Hall, Daishi Hall, Benzaiten Hall, Nyoirin Hall, Guest Hall, and the kitchen area.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1041 Visitors are allowed to ring the temple bell freely.Kinki Nippon Tourist Publishing Center (2005): p. 103 The Main Hall was built in 1836.[http://www.takenaka.co.jp/majorworks/30050111836-01.html?keepThis=true&TB_iframe=true (Takenaka Corporation)] === Principal deity === The temple enshrines the [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Faced Kannon]] as its principal deity. The statue is revealed during the {{ill|Ofune Matsuri|ja|御船祭|Ofune Matsuri=Ofune Matsuri|lt=Ofune Festival|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ofune Matsuri|qq=}}. According to legend, the Kannon statue was a golden Buddha that appeared on the back of a whale from the sea off {{ill|Ousatsu|ja|相差村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ousatsu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (present-day [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]], Osatsu). The site where the whale surfaced is known as "Whale Cape," and the whale itself is said to have turned to stone, now referred to as "Whale Stone."{{Cite web|url=http://www.mietv.com/yajikita09/archive/090408/04.html|title=Whale Cape Promenade|publisher=Mie TV|access-date=May 15, 2011}} The statue was later visited by a high-Ranking monk known as Baramon Sōjō, who carved a wooden image to enshrine the luminous statue within.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1040 == History == === Legendary Origins === According to the "Shōfuku-ji Engi," the temple was founded during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] by [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]]. During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era (749–757), Gyōki received a divine oracle at [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], instructing him to build a temple at "Tenrō Mine" (青峯), promising boundless spiritual benefits.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1039 In 806 CE, [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] returned from Tang China and, in 807 CE, under imperial edict, converted the temple to the [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon sect]]. === Decline and Revival === After periods of decline, the temple was restored during the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享年間|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōkyō era|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} by the monk Ben’i. The belief that "pilgrimage to Aonomine protects against storms" spread, leading to a revival supported by the maritime trade of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Shōfuku-ji reached its peak during the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1831), supported by merchants and seafarers from regions like Owari, Osaka, Ise, and Toba. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1125 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister [[:en:Justin Trudeau|Justin Trudeau]] visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the [[:en:42nd G7 summit|42nd G7 Summit]] in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either [[:en:Matsuo Station|Matsuo Station]] or [[:en:Kutsukake Station|Kutsukake Station]], both served by [[:en:Shima Line|Kintetsu Shima Line]]. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. {{translated page|ja|正福寺 (鳥羽市)|version=103349905|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Shingon Buddhism]] }} [[sn:Shoufukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[ja:正福寺 (鳥羽市)]] [[sn:houfukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[Category:三重四国八十八箇所]] [[Category:Fisheries of Mie prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal Tangible Cultural Properties in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Water transport in Japan]] [[Category:東海三十六不動尊霊場]] [[Category:Edo period architecture]] [[Category:Kōyasan Shingon temples]] [[Category:Buildings in Toba, Mie]] [[Category:History of Toba, Mie]] {{Commons category|Shōfuku-ji (Toba)}}

revision.comment: Bot: prepend Wikidata AFC comment via pages.txt

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11545590]] Wikidata name: Shōfuku-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{infobox religious building | name = Shōfuku-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Shouhukuji (Toba,Mie) 02.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall | location = [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] Matsuo-cho 519 | LatitudeDegrees = 34 | LatitudeMinutes = 24 | LatitudeSeconds = 37.4 | LongitudeDegrees = 136 | LongitudeMinutes = 49 | LongitudeSeconds = 35.5 | Map = Japan#Japan Mie | Mountainname = Aonomine-san | Sect = [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhism]] | Subsect = Kōyasan Shingon | TempleRank = | Principaldeity = [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Faced Kannon]] | Yearconsecration_year = During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era | Founder = [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]] (traditionally attributed) | Restorer = During the Jōkyō era | Alias = Aonomine-san, Saga Palace | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|伊勢西国三十三所観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=}} (Non-numbered Temple)
{{ill|Tōkai-Kinki Jizō Pilgrimage|ja|東海近畿地蔵霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai-Kinki Jizō Pilgrimage|qq=}} (No. 17)
{{ill|Tōkai 36 Fudō Pilgrimage|ja|東海三十六不動尊霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai 36 Fudō Pilgrimage|qq=}} (No. 23)
{{ill|Mie Shikoku 88 Temples|ja|三重四国八十八箇所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Shikoku 88 Temples|qq=}} (No. 88) | CulturalProperty = Two Toba City-designated cultural properties | OfficialWebsite = }} '''Shōfuku-ji''' (正福寺) is a Buddhist temple of the [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon]] sect, located in [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. Its mountain name is Aonomine-san (青峯山), situated near the summit of a 336-meter-high mountain of the same name.{{Cite web|url=https://isekannon.jp/wp-content/themes/isesaigoku2020/files/images/common/panf.pdf|title=Ise Saigoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|publisher=Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage Office|access-date=December 17, 2024}} It is also known by the alias "Saga Palace." Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 703 The temple is a sacred site for maritime safety, attracting fervent devotion from those connected to the fishing industry.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 818 Worshippers range from [[:en:Obira, Hokkaido|Obira Town]], Hokkaido, to [[:en:Kagoshima|Kagoshima City]], Kagoshima.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 706 == Overview == Shōfuku-ji is located in a valley slightly below the peak of Aonomine-san (known as Tenato-yama) at the border of [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]] and [[:en:Shima, Mie|Shima City]]. Commonly referred to as "Aonomine-san," it is a rare [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhist temple]] in the {{ill|Shima Peninsula|ja|志摩地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shima Peninsula|qq=}}, an area dominated by [[:en:Sōtō|Sōtō Zen]] temples.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 999 === Grounds === Within the mountain gate (constructed over 30 years, according to legend), there are several buildings, including the Main Hall (Kondō), Shōten Hall, Daishi Hall, Benzaiten Hall, Nyoirin Hall, Guest Hall, and the kitchen area.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1041 Visitors are allowed to ring the temple bell freely.Kinki Nippon Tourist Publishing Center (2005): p. 103 The Main Hall was built in 1836.[http://www.takenaka.co.jp/majorworks/30050111836-01.html?keepThis=true&TB_iframe=true (Takenaka Corporation)] === Principal deity === The temple enshrines the [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Faced Kannon]] as its principal deity. The statue is revealed during the {{ill|Ofune Matsuri|ja|御船祭|Ofune Matsuri=Ofune Matsuri|lt=Ofune Festival|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ofune Matsuri|qq=}}. According to legend, the Kannon statue was a golden Buddha that appeared on the back of a whale from the sea off {{ill|Ousatsu|ja|相差村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ousatsu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (present-day [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]], Osatsu). The site where the whale surfaced is known as "Whale Cape," and the whale itself is said to have turned to stone, now referred to as "Whale Stone."{{Cite web|url=http://www.mietv.com/yajikita09/archive/090408/04.html|title=Whale Cape Promenade|publisher=Mie TV|access-date=May 15, 2011}} The statue was later visited by a high-Ranking monk known as Baramon Sōjō, who carved a wooden image to enshrine the luminous statue within.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1040 == History == === Legendary Origins === According to the "Shōfuku-ji Engi," the temple was founded during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] by [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]]. During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era (749–757), Gyōki received a divine oracle at [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], instructing him to build a temple at "Tenrō Mine" (青峯), promising boundless spiritual benefits.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1039 In 806 CE, [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] returned from Tang China and, in 807 CE, under imperial edict, converted the temple to the [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon sect]]. === Decline and Revival === After periods of decline, the temple was restored during the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享年間|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōkyō era|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} by the monk Ben’i. The belief that "pilgrimage to Aonomine protects against storms" spread, leading to a revival supported by the maritime trade of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Shōfuku-ji reached its peak during the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1831), supported by merchants and seafarers from regions like Owari, Osaka, Ise, and Toba. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1125 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister [[:en:Justin Trudeau|Justin Trudeau]] visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the [[:en:42nd G7 summit|42nd G7 Summit]] in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either [[:en:Matsuo Station|Matsuo Station]] or [[:en:Kutsukake Station|Kutsukake Station]], both served by [[:en:Shima Line|Kintetsu Shima Line]]. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. {{translated page|ja|正福寺 (鳥羽市)|version=103349905|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Shingon Buddhism]] }} [[sn:Shoufukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[ja:正福寺 (鳥羽市)]] [[sn:houfukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[Category:Mie-Shikoku 88 Temple Pilgrimage]] [[Category:Fisheries of Mie prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal Tangible Cultural Properties in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Water transport in Japan]] [[Category:Tokai 36 Fudō Pilgrimage Sites]] [[Category:Edo period architecture]] [[Category:Kōyasan Shingon temples]] [[Category:Buildings in Toba, Mie]] [[Category:History of Toba, Mie]] {{Commons category|Shōfuku-ji (Toba)}}

The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1125 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister [[:en:Justin Trudeau|Justin Trudeau]] visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the [[:en:42nd G7 summit|42nd G7 Summit]] in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either [[:en:Matsuo Station|Matsuo Station]] or [[:en:Kutsukake Station|Kutsukake Station]], both served by [[:en:Shima Line|Kintetsu Shima Line]]. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. {{translated page|ja|正福寺 (鳥羽市)|version=103349905|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Shingon Buddhism]] }} [[sn:Shoufukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[ja:正福寺 (鳥羽市)]] [[sn:houfukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[Category:Mie-Shikoku 88 Temple Pilgrimage]] [[Category:Fisheries of Mie prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal Tangible Cultural Properties in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Water transport in Japan]] [[Category:Tokai 36 Fudō Pilgrimage Sites]] [[Category:Edo period architecture]] [[Category:Kōyasan Shingon temples]] [[Category:Buildings in Toba, Mie]] [[Category:History of Toba, Mie]] {{Commons category|Shōfuku-ji (Toba)}}

{{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Shouhukuji (Toba,Mie) 02.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Kōyasan Shingon-shū|qid=Q3077357}}|prefecture={{ill|Toba|qid=Q819629}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.410389|N|136.826528|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Gyōki|qid=Q1136664}}|deity={{ill|Eleven-Faced Avalokiteśvara|qid=Q11404731}}|part_of={{ill|Q11357685|qid=Q11357685}}; {{ill|Q11379122|qid=Q11379122}}; {{ill|Q11527791|qid=Q11527791}}; {{ill|Q16482469|qid=Q16482469}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11545590]] Wikidata name: Shōfuku-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{infobox religious building | name = Shōfuku-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Shouhukuji (Toba,Mie) 02.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall | location = [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] Matsuo-cho 519 | LatitudeDegrees = 34 | LatitudeMinutes = 24 | LatitudeSeconds = 37.4 | LongitudeDegrees = 136 | LongitudeMinutes = 49 | LongitudeSeconds = 35.5 | Map = Japan#Japan Mie | Mountainname = Aonomine-san | Sect = [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhism]] | Subsect = Kōyasan Shingon | TempleRank = | Principaldeity = [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Faced Kannon]] | Yearconsecration_year = During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era | Founder = [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]] (traditionally attributed) | Restorer = During the Jōkyō era | Alias = Aonomine-san, Saga Palace | Pilgrimage = {{ill|Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|伊勢西国三十三所観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=|1=Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379122}} (Non-numbered Temple)
{{ill|Tōkai-Kinki Jizō Pilgrimage|ja|東海近畿地蔵霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai-Kinki Jizō Pilgrimage|qq=|1=Tōkai-Kinki Jizō Pilgrimage|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16482469}} (No. 17)
{{ill|Tōkai 36 Fudō Pilgrimage|ja|東海三十六不動尊霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai 36 Fudō Pilgrimage|qq=|1=Tōkai 36 Fudō Pilgrimage|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527791}} (No. 23)
{{ill|Mie Shikoku 88 Temples|ja|三重四国八十八箇所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Shikoku 88 Temples|qq=|1=Mie Shikoku 88 Pilgrimage|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11357685}} (No. 88) | CulturalProperty = Two Toba City-designated cultural properties | OfficialWebsite = }} '''Shōfuku-ji''' (正福寺) is a Buddhist temple of the [[:en:Kōyasan Shingon-shū|Kōyasan Shingon]] sect, located in [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. Its mountain name is Aonomine-san (青峯山), situated near the summit of a 336-meter-high mountain of the same name.{{Cite web|url=https://isekannon.jp/wp-content/themes/isesaigoku2020/files/images/common/panf.pdf|title=Ise Saigoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|publisher=Ise Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage Office|access-date=December 17, 2024}} It is also known by the alias "Saga Palace." Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 703 The temple is a sacred site for maritime safety, attracting fervent devotion from those connected to the fishing industry.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 818 Worshippers range from [[:en:Obira, Hokkaido|Obira Town]], Hokkaido, to [[:en:Kagoshima|Kagoshima City]], Kagoshima.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 706 == Overview == Shōfuku-ji is located in a valley slightly below the peak of Aonomine-san (known as Tenato-yama) at the border of [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]] and [[:en:Shima, Mie|Shima City]]. Commonly referred to as "Aonomine-san," it is a rare [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhist temple]] in the {{ill|Shima Peninsula|ja|志摩地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shima Peninsula|qq=|1=Shima region|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11490920}}, an area dominated by [[:en:Sōtō|Sōtō Zen]] temples.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 999 === Grounds === Within the mountain gate (constructed over 30 years, according to legend), there are several buildings, including the Main Hall (Kondō), Shōten Hall, Daishi Hall, Benzaiten Hall, Nyoirin Hall, Guest Hall, and the kitchen area.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1041 Visitors are allowed to ring the temple bell freely.Kinki Nippon Tourist Publishing Center (2005): p. 103 The Main Hall was built in 1836.[http://www.takenaka.co.jp/majorworks/30050111836-01.html?keepThis=true&TB_iframe=true (Takenaka Corporation)] === Principal deity === The temple enshrines the [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Faced Kannon]] as its principal deity. The statue is revealed during the {{ill|Ofune Matsuri|ja|御船祭|Ofune Matsuri=Ofune Matsuri|lt=Ofune Festival|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ofune Matsuri|qq=}}. According to legend, the Kannon statue was a golden Buddha that appeared on the back of a whale from the sea off {{ill|Ousatsu|ja|相差村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ousatsu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (present-day [[:en:Toba, Mie|Toba City]], Osatsu). The site where the whale surfaced is known as "Whale Cape," and the whale itself is said to have turned to stone, now referred to as "Whale Stone."{{Cite web|url=http://www.mietv.com/yajikita09/archive/090408/04.html|title=Whale Cape Promenade|publisher=Mie TV|access-date=May 15, 2011}} The statue was later visited by a high-Ranking monk known as Baramon Sōjō, who carved a wooden image to enshrine the luminous statue within.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1040 == History == === Legendary Origins === According to the "Shōfuku-ji Engi," the temple was founded during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] by [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]]. During the Tenpyō-Shōhō era (749–757), Gyōki received a divine oracle at [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], instructing him to build a temple at "Tenrō Mine" (青峯), promising boundless spiritual benefits.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1039 In 806 CE, [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] returned from Tang China and, in 807 CE, under imperial edict, converted the temple to the [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon sect]]. === Decline and Revival === After periods of decline, the temple was restored during the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享年間|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōkyō era|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} by the monk Ben’i. The belief that "pilgrimage to Aonomine protects against storms" spread, leading to a revival supported by the maritime trade of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Shōfuku-ji reached its peak during the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1831), supported by merchants and seafarers from regions like Owari, Osaka, Ise, and Toba. The temple’s stone lantern, inscribed with “Maritime Safety, Tenpō 8 (1837),” is a significant cultural artifact.Toba City Historical Compilation Office (1991): p. 1125 === Modern Period === The faith surrounding Shōfuku-ji has evolved, with more individual family visits replacing group pilgrimages.{{Cite web|url=http://balat.jp/pdf/2004_ama.pdf|title=The Life of Ama Divers|publisher=Nagoya City University|access-date=2004}} On May 25, 2016, Canadian Prime Minister [[:en:Justin Trudeau|Justin Trudeau]] visited the temple with his wife Sophie to celebrate their wedding anniversary during the [[:en:42nd G7 summit|42nd G7 Summit]] in Ise-Shima.{{Cite news|title=Canadian PM Visits Aonomine|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=May 26, 2016}} == Cultural Properties == Shōfuku-ji has two Toba City-designated cultural properties: 1. The Grand Gate (Edo period, 1830). 2. A 7-meter stone lantern inscribed “Maritime Safety.” == Access == To reach the temple, visitors can hike from either [[:en:Matsuo Station|Matsuo Station]] or [[:en:Kutsukake Station|Kutsukake Station]], both served by [[:en:Shima Line|Kintetsu Shima Line]]. For those driving, access is via the Matsuo Route or the Motoya Route, with parking available near the temple gate. {{translated page|ja|正福寺 (鳥羽市)|version=103349905|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Shingon Buddhism]] }} [[sn:Shoufukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[ja:正福寺 (鳥羽市)]] [[sn:houfukuji Temple (Toba City)]] [[Category:Mie-Shikoku 88 Temple Pilgrimage]] [[Category:Fisheries of Mie prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal Tangible Cultural Properties in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Water transport in Japan]] [[Category:Tokai 36 Fudō Pilgrimage Sites]] [[Category:Edo period architecture]] [[Category:Kōyasan Shingon temples]] [[Category:Buildings in Toba, Mie]] [[Category:History of Toba, Mie]] {{Commons category|Shōfuku-ji (Toba)}} {{wikidata link|Q11545590}}
